project.title: NYC — API & Agent Modernization
project.tagline: Mapping the digital surface of New York City government and turning it into an API-first, agent-native layer — one JSON Schema per object, an OpenAPI that $refs them, and an MCP server per domain.

thesis.heading: Not another data-liberation project
thesis.body: From 2010–2018 the open-data movement (Socrata, Obama-era directives, NYC's Open Data law) pushed cities to publish data. It only partially worked: data got published but stayed disconnected from the front door, read-only, unproductized, and not agent-ready. This project is step two — unify what exists behind resource-oriented APIs, complete the missing write workflows, and expose it all through MCP so agents are first-class consumers. Make the city programmable.

method.d: Outside-in crawl; index every table, form, and data file as name · type · URL · entity.
Fingerprint the CMS, hosting, CDN, analytics, and any outsourced capabilities.
Document every backend/service API the site calls while crawling.
Match each resource to existing data.cityofnewyork.us datasets; separate gaps from disconnects.
One canonical schema file per entity, reconciling website columns with Open Data schemas.
A resource-oriented contract that $refs each object schema; reads over unified data, writes for missing workflows.
Expose the same resources as agent tools (design artifact, not a deployment).

inventory.note: Distinct registrable domains/subdomains catalogued; the true surface is larger (~100 agencies mostly under nyc.gov/site/* paths, plus hundreds of aNNN-*.nyc.gov app hosts).

domains.agency: New York City Department of Parks & Recreation (NYC Parks)
domains.platform: Smarty 2.6.2 / jQuery (legacy PHP)
domains.tagline: Data-rich but rendered as HTML; a legacy delivery layer with one modern island (the Next.js Tree Map). Modernize by replatforming behind a resource API.
domains.netnew: PermitApplication
findings: Rich structured data is published as HTML tables, div-based record listings, and PDFs.
ZERO machine-readable downloads (CSV, XLS, XLSX, XML, JSON, GeoJSON) were found linked anywhere on the domain during sampling.
The sitemap.xml declared in robots.txt returns the 404 page (broken sitemap).
Legacy backends surface directly in URLs (e.g. .php endpoints under /sub_your_park/).
There is no developer/API/open-data page on the domain; the interactive Tree Map (tree-map.nycgovparks.org) is the only app-like data surface.

apis_observed.note: Next.js app backend; ~650k trees; undocumented.
Self-hosted; no vendor (unlike DOE HawkSearch).
Historical Signs/Monuments backend.
image delivery/transform.
alerts.
page translation.
Tree Map telemetry.
237 DPR assets; disconnected from site.

domains.agency: New York City Public Schools (NYCPS / Department of Education, DOE)
domains.platform: Progress Sitefinity (.NET)
domains.tagline: 638 Open Data assets, yet search is rented to a vendor (HawkSearch) and the backend is hidden behind an internal API. Modernize by reclaiming those capabilities.
domains.netnew: EnrollmentApplication
findings: 3,343 individual school pages (per the sitemap) form the school directory — each a structured School record rendered client-side.
On-domain the data is NOT machine-readable; but NYC Open Data has 638 DOE assets including 462-column High School Directories, School Locations, and Demographic Snapshots.
Site search / 'Find a School' is outsourced to a vendor (HawkSearch) — DOE does not own a public school-search API.
School pages are driven by an internal /CustomApi/* backend that is robots-disallowed, undocumented, and not public.
The enrollment/application workflow (MySchools) is transactional and has no Open Data twin — the net-new API opportunity, analogous to Parks permits.
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md for the vendor and backend-API inventory.

apis_observed.note: Powers Find-a-School and site search.
robots-disallowed; drives page/school data.

school locator maps.
per-dataset; disconnected from site.

domains.agency: New York City Council (NYCC)
domains.platform: WordPress / WP Engine
domains.tagline: The most API-covered domain — three APIs (Legistar, WordPress REST, Open Data), none owned or unified. Modernize by consolidating them behind one owned contract.
domains.netnew: TestimonyRegistration
findings: Council is the most API-covered domain so far: a vendor Legistar Web API (webapi.legistar.com/v1/nyc), an OPEN WordPress REST API (council.nyc.gov/wp-json), and 11 NYCC Open Data datasets.
Yet none is an owned, unified, agent-native NYC Council API — the legislative record depends on a Granicus-branded, access-gated vendor API (403 to our client).
51 district/member pages (/district-N/), 40 committees, 9 caucuses, 47 reports — content in WordPress; bills/hearings/votes in Legistar.
Open Data has Members, Legislation (Bills & Local Laws), Discretionary Funding, Participatory Budgeting, Meetings, Committee Membership — flattened snapshots disconnected from Legistar and the site.
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md for the vendor + API inventory.

apis_observed.note: bodies/matters/persons/events/votes; vendor-branded, access-gated.
open; content (posts/pages/media/nycc_feature), not legislative resources.
public bill/hearing/vote search UI.
11 NYCC datasets; flattened snapshots.

domains.agency: New York City Board of Elections (BOENY)
domains.platform: Drupal 9
domains.tagline: The least-modernized: no site API, only 2 Open Data assets; results and candidates are PDFs. Modernize by digitizing the data that does not yet exist.
domains.netnew: BallotRequest
findings: The least-modernized domain in the project: Drupal 9 with JSON:API disabled (no site API), and only 2 Open Data assets (poll sites).

The two things voters most need — candidates (who's running) and results — have NO API and NO dataset; they are PDFs (one Recap + one EDLevel PDF per contest) plus a separate legacy viewer (enr.boenyc.gov).
Poll sites ARE machine-readable: Voting/Poll Sites (29 cols) + Election District Poll Sites (4 cols) on Open Data; election district geometry via DCP.
Ballot request/tracking is a separate app (requestballot.vote.nyc) with no public API.
This is the original pre-open-data low-hanging-fruit case, largely untouched — the sharpest validation of 'data liberation only partially worked.'
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 404 — disabled; no content API.
separate legacy app; no documented data API.
no public API.
only 2 BOENY datasets (poll sites).

domains.agency: NYC311 (Office of Technology and Innovation / 311)
domains.platform: Microsoft Dynamics 365
domains.tagline: The flagship 311 dataset is the most-used in the city, but the interactive Open311 standard NYC once ran is retired and the service is a vendor CRM. Modernize by reviving the open standard.
domains.netnew: ServiceRequest (Open311)
findings: Fifth distinct platform: Microsoft Dynamics 365 / Power Apps Portals on Azure — the city's front door is a vendor CRM.
The 311 data is the most-used in the city: 311 Service Requests (erm2-nwe9) has 1.26M views / 590k downloads and a live SODA endpoint over tens of millions of records.
But the interactive service API is retired: NYC was an early Open311 (GeoReport v2) adopter and the standard endpoints (311api.cityofnewyork.us) no longer resolve.
A real API gateway exists (api.nyc.gov, Azure APIM) but is scoped to GeoClient geocoding and key-gated (401) — not 311.
5-for-5 on the universal gap: submitting/tracking a 311 request has no public API (Open311 lapsed; Dynamics has no public write API).
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: flagship 311 dataset; live.
GeoClient geocoding (401); nearest thing to a central NYC gateway.
endpoints no longer resolve.
report/check-status; internal.

domains.agency: NYC Department of Buildings (DOB)
domains.platform: aNNN-*.nyc.gov legacy app layer (BIS/DOB NOW, Java/Tomcat) behind Akamai
domains.tagline: A mature transactional core (BIS Web, DOB NOW) with no public API — only a nightly one-way Open Data batch dump. The app layer IS the legacy surface. Modernize by fronting it with one owned live API + a permit-filing write.
domains.netnew: PermitApplication
findings: The real legacy surface is the APPLICATION LAYER, not the website. www.nyc.gov/site/buildings is brochureware; the transactional systems of record are the aNNN-*.nyc.gov apps — BIS Web (a810-bisweb, legacy Building Information System), DOB NOW (a810-dobnow, the filing/permitting portal), and eFiling (a810-efiling, Apache Tomcat/Java).
None of the app-layer systems exposes a public API. BIS Web and DOB NOW are browser-only and Akamai-gated (Access Denied to non-browser clients).
The ONLY machine-readable output is a nightly Socrata BATCH DUMP. Every DOB dataset carries a 'DOBRunDate' column proving it is a one-way nightly extract, not a live interface.

44 DOB Open Data datasets, led by hugely popular ones: DOB Job Application Filings (ic3t-wcy2, 2.4M views, 95 cols), DOB Permit Issuance (ipu4-2q9a, 604k), DOB NOW Approved Permits (rbx6-tga4, 273k), DOB Complaints (eabe-havv), DOB Violations (3h2n-5cm9), DOB ECB Violations (6bgk-3dad), DOB Certificate of Occupancy (bs8b-p36w).

The domain is data-rich but transaction-closed: you can read yesterday's filings/permits/violations, but you cannot file, pay, or check status of a DOB NOW permit application via any API — that is the net-new write surface.
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md for the platform + app-layer inventory.

apis_observed.note: 44 DOB datasets; nightly BATCH extract of BIS/DOB NOW (DOBRunDate column). The only machine-readable DOB surface — read-only, lossy, a day stale.
The modern transactional system for filings, permits, C of O, elevator/boiler/facade safety. No public API; 403 'Access Denied' to non-browser clients.
Legacy Building Information System — the pre-DOB NOW system of record for jobs, permits, violations, complaints. Browser-only.
Apache Tomcat/9.0.117 (Java) — DOB eFiling. No API.
Brochureware on the citywide NYC.gov CMS (nginx + Akamai edge; Dynatrace + mPulse RUM). Links out to the app layer; holds no records.
Google Website Translator on the CMS.
City Record Online and NYC notification apps referenced from the DOB pages — same aNNN legacy-app pattern.

domains.agency: NYC Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD)
domains.platform: Angular SPA over a private owned REST API (WSO2 gateway) + NYC GeoSearch
domains.tagline: HPD already runs a modern, owned, versioned REST API — kept private behind one Angular app while the public surface is 47 flattened snapshots and a closed lottery. Modernize by exposing what exists.
domains.netnew: HousingLotteryApplication
findings: HPD has already BUILT a modern, owned backend REST API — mspwvw-hpdleov3.nyc.gov/hpdonline.api/1.0/api — behind a WSO2 API gateway (*.hpdnyc.org:8243), with NYC GeoSearch integration. But it is private, undocumented, and serves only the HPD Online Angular SPA.

The public machine-readable surface is instead 47 NYC Open Data datasets (Housing Maintenance Code Violations at 215k+ views leads) — flattened periodic snapshots describing the same objects/keys as the private live API.

The highest-value citizen transaction — applying to an affordable-housing lottery on NYC Housing Connect — is a closed ASP.NET silo with NO public API; only read-only 'advertised lotteries' Open Data twins exist.

Entities recover cleanly from both the SPA and Open Data, joined by buildingId / registrationId / BBL / BIN: Building, Violation, Complaint (+problems), Registration (+contacts), Litigation, AffordableHousingProject.
Fourth distinct modernization pattern: not replatform (Parks), reclaim (DOE), or consolidate (Council) — but EXPOSE an owned API that already exists. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: The live HPD record (buildings, violations, complaints, registrations, litigation, charges, bedbug, vacate). Owned .nyc.gov host, versioned /1.0; hard-coded as apiBaseURL in HPD Online main.js. Private/undocumented.
documentApiBaseURL; serves NOV/order PDFs via /documents/content.
Port 8243 = WSO2 gateway default; whitelisted in the SPA CSP connect-src. HPD already runs API management internally.
47 HPD datasets; flattened periodic snapshots of the same data.
Address autocomplete -> BBL/BIN/coords; used by HPD Online property lookup.
Enterprise GIS map referenced by the SPA.
Housing Connect 2; affordable-housing applications are UI-only, no public API.
GA4 G-GYRKWPJBCL.

domains.agency: New York City Department of Transportation (DOT)
domains.platform: Legacy .shtml on nyc.gov + Drupal (nycstreetdesign.info); Socrata data
domains.tagline: More open data than any NYC domain (267 assets) but as scattered flat snapshots + map-only visualizations keyed to a street SegmentID never exposed as a queryable network API.
domains.netnew: StreetWorkPermit
findings: DOT is the most open-data-rich domain in the project: 267 verified Open Data assets under agency label 'Department of Transportation (DOT)'.
But it is 178 flat datasets + 64 MAP-ONLY visualizations (zero queryable columns) + 18 external links — scattered snapshots, no owned or unified DOT API.
Nearly every street asset carries a LION SegmentID (bike routes, traffic counts, closures), yet DOT exposes no queryable street-network API keyed on it.
The one transaction — applying for a street-work permit — runs through the NYCSTREET portal (nycstreets.net) with NO public API; only the issued-permit snapshot (tqtj-sjs8) reaches Open Data.
Two disconnected web stacks: legacy .shtml main site (Akamai/AWS/Dynatrace) and a modern Drupal 9 street-design microsite on Pantheon.
Motor Vehicle Collisions datasets are NYPD, not DOT; DOT's safety layer is Vision Zero View (v7f4-yzyg). See apis-observed.md and tech-stack.md.

apis_observed.note: Per-dataset SODA/GeoJSON for all 267 DOT assets; flat snapshots + map-only visualizations; no cross-dataset resource model or SegmentID-joined network.
Used to enumerate DOT inventory by Dataset-Information_Agency.
Street-opening/construction permit applications; NO public API. The net-new write surface.
Street Design Guidelines; JSON:API-capable but serves manual content, not street assets.

domains.agency: NYC Department of Health and Mental Hygiene (DOHMH)
domains.platform: NYC.gov CMS over Accela COTS + ASP.NET apps on a816-*.nyc.gov
domains.tagline: The most-liberated data in the city (81 datasets incl. restaurant inspections), yet transactions — ordering a birth/death certificate — are locked in siloed legacy apps with no API.
domains.netnew: VitalRecordRequest
findings: DOHMH is the most DATA-RICH domain in the project: 81 datasets on NYC Open Data, led by 'DOHMH New York City Restaurant Inspection Results' (43nn-pn8j, ~406k views) — among the single most-viewed datasets in all of NYC Open Data.

But its TRANSACTIONAL services are siloed in separate legacy apps with no open, agent-native API: ABC Eats (restaurant grade lookup; AngularJS + ASP.NET MVC), eVital VRRTS + VitalChek (birth/death certificate orders), and Accela Citizen Access (health permits & licenses).

The public restaurant grade lookup (ABC Eats) is a JavaScript SPA over a private Web API — the same famous data that is ALSO fully open on Socrata, so the read need is met twice while the write/transaction need is met zero times.
The one true net-new WRITE surface is ordering a vital record (birth/death certificate): a paid transaction locked inside eVital/VitalChek with no open API.
Reframe: Parks=replatform, DOE=reclaim, Council=consolidate+own, DOHMH=TRANSACT — the open data is solved; the gap is agent-native, transactional services. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

domains.agency: New York City Department of Sanitation (DSNY)
domains.platform: Oracle WebCenter Sites + React forms over ASP.NET backends
domains.tagline: DSNY already runs first-party lookup and pickup-scheduling APIs — undocumented backends behind React forms, waiting to be surfaced as one owned, agent-native contract.
domains.netnew: BulkPickupRequest
findings: DSNY already runs REAL first-party backend APIs — but every one is undocumented and reachable only by reverse-engineering the DSNY React forms: dsnypublic.nyc.gov/dsny/api (geocoder/DSNYCollection collection-schedule lookup, geocoder/BCW), the ePickups API (ePickupsAPI/api/PickupRequest/AddUpdatePickUpRequest, GetUnavailableDates, IsDistrictActive), and the CFC appointment API (dsnydonate.nyc.gov/cfc/api/appointment).

So the net-new WRITE object — BulkPickupRequest (schedule a bulk/appointment pickup) — is not truly net-new capability; the ePickups backend already does it. What is net-new is an OWNED, documented, agent-native contract over it.

51 DSNY datasets on NYC Open Data (356K lifetime views), led by DSNY Monthly Tonnage (ebb7-mvp5, 58K), Public Recycling Bins, Recycling Diversion & Capture Rates, and Electronics/Food-Scrap/Special-Waste drop-off locations.

The website is Oracle WebCenter Sites (.page URLs, livesite-version header) with React SPA forms; backends are ASP.NET Web API on IIS behind ARR, with permissive CORS (access-control-allow-origin: *).
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md for the vendor + API inventory.

apis_observed.note: First-party. Resolves an address to a DSNY district + collection schedule; BCW geocodes commercial-waste zones. No docs, no OpenAPI.
First-party ASP.NET Web API. Already schedules bulk/large-item pickups (AppointmentDate/Time/Items, BulkPickup). CORS *. The backend behind the net-new BulkPickupRequest.
First-party CFC/special-waste drop-off appointment booking behind the React forms.
ASP.NET Web API; permissive CORS; serves the DSNY forms/services.
Open JSON content API (200) serving DSNY page/form content.
51 DSNY datasets (tonnage, recycling rates, drop-off sites, litter baskets, districts/zones).
Commercial-waste-zone map layers.
Where missed-collection and sanitation complaints are filed today.
Performance monitoring and page translation.

domains.agency: New York City Police Department (NYPD)
domains.platform: Oracle WebCenter Sites + Angular SPAs on an undocumented Azure Gov backend
domains.tagline: The most data-rich domain, yet its record ships as flattened Open Data snapshots and its live surfaces are trapped in Angular apps on an undocumented backend.
domains.netnew: PoliceReportRequest
findings: NYPD is the most DATA-rich domain in the project: 42 datasets under the Socrata agency label 'Police Department (NYPD)' — including Motor Vehicle Collisions - Crashes (h9gi-nx95), the single most-viewed dataset in the entire NYC Open Data portal.
But that data is published as flattened periodic snapshots on a third-party portal, not a live, queryable, owned NYPD API.

The department's own interactive tools — CompStat 2.0 (compstat.nypdonline.org) and the Officer Profile discipline search (nypdonline.org) — are Angular/Kendo single-page apps talking to an UNDOCUMENTED Azure Government backend (officer.search.azure.us). Real, current data, no public contract.
Three different stacks in one department: Oracle WebCenter Sites for the informational site, a bespoke Angular + Azure Gov application tier for transparency tools.
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md for the platform + API inventory.

apis_observed.note: 42 NYPD datasets (complaints, arrests, shootings, summonses, use of force, officer profiles, sectors, NCOs). Flattened snapshots, not a live incident API.

domains.agency: NYC Taxi & Limousine Commission (TLC)
domains.platform: NYC.gov CMS; Socrata + a CloudFront/S3 parquet trip-record host
domains.tagline: A world-famous open-data producer whose data ships as monthly parquet dumps and flat snapshots, never a queryable, transactional API.
domains.netnew: LicenseApplication
findings: TLC is the most DATA-rich domain in the project and the least API-native: a world-famous open-data producer whose data ships as batch, not as a queryable API.

The flagship TLC Trip Record Data (yellow/green/FHV/FHVHV) is monthly PARQUET files on CloudFront/S3 (d37ci6vzurychx.cloudfront.net/trip-data) — a bulk download host, NOT an API. Only a subset of yearly trip snapshots is mirrored on Socrata.

80 TLC datasets on NYC Open Data (agency 'Taxi and Limousine Commission (TLC)', verified) cover licensed drivers, vehicles, medallion/SHL permits, FHV bases, inspection schedules, and application status — but as flat daily snapshots, one dataset per license class.
The Taxi Zones behind every trip's PULocationID/DOLocationID are a static CSV + shapefile (/misc/taxi_zone_lookup.csv, taxi_zones.zip), not a resource.
Licensing exposes only READ-ONLY status (TLC New Driver Application Status dpec-ucu7); there is no API to APPLY for a TLC license. That is the one net-new write surface.
Website runs on the shared NYC.gov Livesite CMS (nginx + Akamai + AWS ALB + Dynatrace) — a brochure; none of TLC's operational data is served from it. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: TLC Trip Record Data — monthly parquet per service class; download host, not an API. Verified 200, ~50MB, AmazonS3.
taxi_zone_lookup.csv + taxi_zones.zip — the ~263 zones behind trip LocationIDs.
80 TLC datasets; flat daily snapshots; no unified TLC resource model.

domains.agency: New York City Department of City Planning (DCP)
domains.platform: nyc.gov + Netlify Planning Labs apps (ZoLa) on CARTO/Mapbox; open-source geocoder; GeoClient on Azure APIM
domains.tagline: The source of the city's shared geography — BBL, community districts, NTAs, census tracts, council/election boundaries — so it should be the explicit, owned base every other domain's schemas reference.
domains.netnew: — reference agency (owned /geocode)
findings: DCP is the SOURCE of the city's shared geography — PLUTO/MapPLUTO defines the BBL, and DCP defines community districts, NTAs, census tracts, and council/election-district boundaries that Parks, DOE, Council, and BOE datasets all join on. This makes DCP the natural authoritative base for the project's planned nyc-commons shared schemas.

It has the most real engineering of any domain so far — a subscription-gated GeoClient/GeoService (Geosupport) on Azure API Management, modern Netlify/CARTO/Mapbox map apps (ZoLa, FactFinder), and 308 open-source repos — yet still no single owned, resource-oriented, agent-native DCP API.

188 DCP Open Data datasets (PLUTO 108 cols, MapPLUTO, zoning tax lots, community districts, NTAs, census tracts, borough/election geographies, facilities, POPS) — the richest geography catalog in the city, but flat assets plus GitHub release files, not a resource model.

The canonical address->BBL geocoder is reachable only through a subscription-gated, Azure-hosted GeoClient (api.nyc.gov/geo, 401 to our client) — the single highest-value capability, un-owned as a public contract.

DCP is a REFERENCE agency, not a transactional one: there is no obvious citizen write-workflow. Its ULURP land-use process is an agency/applicant pipeline, not a resident transaction. The net-new value is a canonical geocode contract, not a write endpoint.
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md for the platform + API inventory.

apis_observed.note: Canonical address->BBL/BIN + administrative-geography resolver; the city's most-referenced geo capability, behind a vendor gateway + API key.
188 DCP datasets — PLUTO/MapPLUTO, zoning tax lots, community districts, NTAs, census tracts, geographies; flat assets, not a resource model.

domains.agency: New York City Office of the Comptroller
domains.platform: WordPress (Comptroller) + Drupal (Checkbook NYC), both behind Imperva
domains.tagline: Already runs a real public API (Checkbook NYC, XML) + 15 Open Data assets + an eClaim form, but nothing is a unified, JSON-first, agent-native contract.
domains.netnew: ClaimFiling
findings: The Comptroller ALREADY runs a public API — Checkbook NYC (POST checkbooknyc.com/api) — covering Spending, Contracts, Budget, Revenue, and Payroll; probed live at 3.2M spending / 10.2M payroll / 1.2M revenue FY2024 records, no key required.
But it is XML request/response, POST-only, with a bespoke search_criteria/response_columns envelope — no OpenAPI, no JSON Schema, no MCP, not resource-oriented, not agent-native.

The office's data is split across THREE surfaces: Checkbook (spending/contracts), NYC Open Data (15 assets — pension holdings x5, claims report, published audits, bonds, debt service), and the WordPress site (audits/RFPs/eClaim).
checkbooknyc.com is a Drupal app; comptroller.nyc.gov is WordPress; both sit behind Imperva. Comptroller Mark Levine per site metadata.
The one citizen write-workflow with no API is filing a claim against the City (eClaim / file-a-claim) — the net-new write object ClaimFiling.
See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md for the API + vendor inventory.

apis_observed.note: EXISTING public spending API. type_of_data = Spending/Contracts/Budget/Revenue/Payroll. Probed live (Spending 3.2M FY24). XML envelope, POST-only, no OpenAPI/JSON/MCP, vendor Drupal — not agent-native.
open; content (reports, RFPs, events, nyc_bonds), not financial resources; FacetWP search.
15 Comptroller assets — pension holdings (5 systems), claims report, published audits, bonds, debt service; flattened snapshots; top asset is an href to Checkbook.
site search/filtering; not a data API.

domains.agency: New York City Housing Authority (NYCHA)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Livesite + Oracle Siebel CRM Self-Service portal
domains.tagline: Reference data is wide open on Open Data, but every resident transaction is locked inside a vendor Siebel CRM with no API.
domains.netnew: WorkOrder
findings: NYCHA is a split domain: an informational NYC.gov site (Akamai/nginx/Livesite/Dynatrace) plus a resident Self Service Portal that is an Oracle Siebel CRM (selfserve.nycha.info/nycha/app/eservice/enu) with no API.

The reference data is unusually OPEN: 24 NYC Open Data datasets covering developments (Development Data Book, 55 columns), residential addresses, community facilities, six streams of utility consumption-and-cost metering, and aggregate resident demographics.

But the resident SERVICE layer has no machine-readable surface at all — rent, recertification, waitlist applications, and repair work orders live only inside the login-walled, JavaScript-only Siebel portal or a phone call to the Customer Contact Center.
Resident/household data is published only in aggregate (Resident Data Book Summary); NYCHA never exposes an individual HouseholdRecord to Open Data.
Net-new write surface: report a repair (WorkOrder) — the single most common resident transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 24 NYCHA datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open NYCHA API — reference/asset data only.
rent, recertification, application/waitlist status, work orders; server-rendered Siebel Open UI (SWECmd), JavaScript-only, no JSON/OpenAPI.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.

domains.agency: NYC Administration for Children's Services (ACS)
domains.platform: NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' platform (Akamai + nginx + Dynatrace + Google Maps JS); no ACS-owned application — public actions delegated to NY State OCFS (Central Register) and NYC 311
domains.tagline: A confidentiality-bound agency whose only machine-readable asset is a provider directory and whose two public actions are delegated to the State and to 311.
domains.netnew: ChildCareComplaint — submit a complaint about a child care provider (models the provider concern, never case data; abuse routed to the State Central Register)
findings: ACS is a confidentiality-bound agency: abuse/neglect investigations, foster care, and juvenile detention are confidential by statute and published only in aggregate — never as individual cases.
Its Open Data footprint is thin and mostly non-tabular: of 21 ACS assets, 16 are 'file' attachments (uploaded Excel/PDF reports you cannot query) and only 5 are tabular datasets.
Exactly one ACS dataset is address-level and machine-readable — ACS Community Partners (9hyh-zkx9, 39 columns), the provider directory. Everything else is aggregate accountability reporting.

Its two public actions are delegated, not owned: reporting child abuse goes to the NY State OCFS Statewide Central Register (1-800-342-3720), and complaining about a child care provider goes to NYC 311 (portal.311.nyc.gov).

Net-new write surface: submit a child-care-provider complaint (ChildCareComplaint) as an owned ACS contract instead of deferring to 311 — modeling the provider concern, never case data, and redirecting any suspected abuse to the State Central Register. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 21 ACS assets, but only 5 tabular and 1 address-level (Community Partners 9hyh-zkx9); the rest are non-queryable file attachments. The one useful open ACS API.
Where reporting child abuse/neglect actually goes; not an ACS system and deliberately human-mediated, not machine-readable.
Where child care / provider complaints and other ACS-adjacent requests are filed; ACS defers to 311 rather than owning an intake.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM, embedded Google Maps JS. No content API.

domains.agency: New York City Business Integrity Commission (BIC)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Livesite (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) + Salesforce Experience Cloud portal (bicportal.nyc.gov)
domains.tagline: BIC's regulatory registry is the most open of any domain yet, but every business transaction is locked inside a Salesforce portal.
domains.netnew: TradeWasteLicenseApplication — apply for a trade waste license/registration
findings: BIC is a split domain: an informational NYC.gov site (Akamai/nginx/Livesite/Dynatrace) plus a licensing/enforcement portal that is a Salesforce Experience Cloud app (bicportal.nyc.gov, server: sfdcedge) with no API.

The regulatory registry is unusually OPEN: 9 NYC Open Data datasets covering trade waste hauler licensees (867j-5pgi, 78k views, BIC's most-viewed asset), broker/self-hauler/construction-and-demolition registrants, public wholesale-market businesses (Hunts Point, Fulton Fish), the licensee/registrant vehicle fleet, issued violations, complaints, and denied companies.

But the business SERVICE layer has no machine-readable surface: applying for or renewing a license/registration and paying a violation fine (/s/viopay) live only inside the login-walled Salesforce portal or on paper.

The open data publishes the OUTPUTS of the licensing lifecycle (who is licensed, who was denied, what violations issued) but offers no way to TRANSACT it — a public registry with a private front counter.
Net-new write surface: apply for a trade waste license/registration (TradeWasteLicenseApplication) — no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 9 BIC datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open BIC API — regulatory registry, violations, complaints, and fleet data.
apply/renew a license or registration; pay a violation fine (/s/viopay). Salesforce Lightning community — server: sfdcedge, x-sfdc-request-id; JavaScript-only, no JSON/OpenAPI.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
BIC complaints about trade waste hauling / wholesale markets route through 311; only closed records reach Open Data.
x-akamai-transformed on the informational site.
x-oneagent-js-injection real-user monitoring.

domains.agency: Brooklyn Public Library (BPL)
domains.platform: Drupal (Cloudflare) + BiblioCommons ILS + discover.bklynlibrary.org events
domains.tagline: BPL already runs a full, public Drupal JSON:API it never advertised — formalize it, own it, and add the catalog hold write locked inside the BiblioCommons ILS.
domains.netnew: place a hold (BookHold)
findings: BPL is an independent NONPROFIT (not a city agency) running Drupal behind Cloudflare, with the catalog on the BiblioCommons ILS and events on discover.bklynlibrary.org.

The standout finding is the inverse of the usual one: BPL's content data is ALREADY a working public API. The Drupal JSON:API module is fully open at www.bklynlibrary.org/jsonapi — ~22 content types (node--branch, node--event, node--book_profile, node--digital_asset, node--eres, finding aids, and more) served as read-only JSON — real, comprehensive, and entirely UNDOCUMENTED and unadvertised.

Branch data on that API is rich: full address, weekly hours (seconds-from-midnight), geo point, subway/bus lines, holds-pickup flag, and ~40 service fields — far more than the two file-link datasets BPL posts to NYC Open Data.

What has NO owned public API is the catalog transaction layer, locked in the BiblioCommons ILS: searching the bibliographic catalog, the patron account/library card, and — above all — placing a HOLD on an item.

Net-new write surface: place a hold (BookHold) — the everyday library transaction, with no public API today; plus applying for a library card (a Drupal webform). See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: FULLY PUBLIC, read-only. 289 resource links, ~22 node types (branch, event, external_event, book_profile, digital_asset, eres, exhibition, finding_aid, research_guide, podcasts, video, press_release, blog…). Real and comprehensive but undocumented/unadvertised — the accidental API. This is the content surface to formalize.

Bibliographic catalog search, patron account, library card, and holds/reservations. Server-rendered vendor app (SRV/app cookies, select_library redirect). The catalog write layer — placing a hold — has no owned public API.
The public programs/events calendar (bklynlibrary.org/calendar redirects here). Backs node--event; no documented JSON API of its own.
Only 2 BPL-labeled assets (BPL Branches, BPL Electronic Resources), both type=href external links — NOT live SODA tables. BPL, as a nonprofit, has no Open Data mandate.
server: cloudflare, cf-ray, cf-cache-status HIT on the main site and discover subdomain.

domains.agency: Office of the Bronx Borough President
domains.platform: Revize government SaaS CMS (PHP front end + JSP admin at cms2.revize.com), on Oracle Cloud; NOT the shared NYC.gov 'Livesite' platform. Events on Google Calendar; Curator.io + Constant Contact + UserWay + GA4 widgets.
domains.tagline: A thin brochure site on a vendor CMS where only two of the office's charter entities are machine-readable — and where the fix is one shared Borough President API, not a bespoke Bronx build.
domains.netnew: CommunityBoardApplication — apply to serve on one of the Bronx's twelve community boards (today a PDF/email; no structured intake or status).
findings: The Bronx Borough President site (bronxboropres.nyc.gov) is a thin brochure site on the Revize government SaaS CMS — PHP front end, JSP CMS back end, hosted on Oracle Cloud, NOT on the shared NYC.gov 'Livesite' platform. It exposes no API and no OpenAPI.

Only TWO of the office's entities are machine-readable at all — as NYC Open Data datasets under the label 'Bronx Borough President (BPBX)': Bronx Borough President Capital Funding (mdgu-ar69, 20 columns) and Bronx Community Boards (wbau-xy7g, 7 columns).

The office's charter-mandated work is otherwise trapped in unstructured surfaces: ULURP land-use recommendations are PDFs in a Revize 'document center', the newsroom (releases, statements, letters, testimonies) is server-rendered PHP pages, and events live in two imported Google Calendars.

Constituent-facing intake is analog: applying to serve on a community board is a downloadable PDF / email; there is no structured application and no way to check status. That is the net-new WRITE surface.

All FIVE Borough President offices carry the same NYC Charter duties (ULURP advisory recommendations, community-board appointments, a share of discretionary capital/expense funding, chairing the borough board) and run near-identical thin sites on assorted vendor CMSes. This is a candidate for ONE shared, templatable Borough President API instantiated per borough — not five bespoke builds. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Exactly 2 datasets under 'Bronx Borough President (BPBX)': Capital Funding (mdgu-ar69) and Bronx Community Boards (wbau-xy7g). SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The only real, open API surface this office has.

Two imported calendars — 'Events' and 'Meetings' — surfaced through the Revize calendar plugin on calendar.php. Not an owned API.

domains.agency: Office of the Bronx District Attorney (Darcel D. Clark)
domains.platform: Legacy NYC.gov /html/ SHTML site — server-side-includes (.shtml) pages, a JavaScript-built nav (nav-nodes.js/top-nav.js) and jQuery/Bootstrap/Tether; Akamai edge + AWS ALB origin + Dynatrace and Akamai mPulse RUM + Google Analytics/Translate. Aggregate prosecution data is embedded from Microsoft Power BI (Gov) (app.powerbigov.us). NOT the shared NYC.gov 'Livesite' platform.
domains.tagline: A legacy SHTML brochure site with no API and zero Open Data, where the only prosecution data the office publishes is locked inside Power BI iframes — and where the real fix is one shared District Attorney API across all five boroughs.
domains.netnew: TipSubmission — a structured, trackable intake for a crime tip, a Civilian Complaint Unit complaint of police misconduct, or a FOIL records request (today a phone call to 718-590-2300 or an email to FOILREQUEST@BRONXDA.NYC.GOV, with no tracking number).
findings: The Bronx DA exposes NO API and has ZERO NYC Open Data datasets — the Socrata Discovery API returns 0 for every agency label. Prosecution is a county function outside the Open Data program.
The public site is a legacy NYC.gov '/html/' SHTML application (SSI pages, JavaScript-built nav, jQuery/Bootstrap) behind Akamai and an AWS ALB — not the shared 'Livesite' chassis.

The office's only quantitative public data — arrests, charging decisions, case outcomes, defendant demographics — is trapped inside Microsoft Power BI (Gov) iframes: rendered pixels with no download, no query endpoint, and no Open Data twin.

There is no write surface: crime tips and Civilian Complaint Unit complaints are a phone call (718-590-2300) and FOIL requests are a plain email (FOILREQUEST@BRONXDA.NYC.GOV), neither returning a tracking number.

All five NYC borough DA offices run the same charter functions, so the low-hanging fruit is ONE shared District Attorney API (see OfficeReference in the schemas), not a bespoke Bronx build. Net-new write surface: submit a tip/complaint/FOIL request. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Socrata Discovery API returns 0 datasets for every Bronx DA agency label. The county-prosecutor function sits outside NYC Open Data.

The /html/data/ dashboards (arrests, charging decisions, case outcomes, defendant demographics; report 'Public Dash Case v9a') are iframe embeds — rendered pixels, no CSV/JSON/query endpoint, no Open Data twin. The office's only quantitative public data.
Server-side-includes .shtml pages; JS-built nav (nav-nodes.js). No content API, no JSON, no OpenAPI.
Real-user monitoring snippet on every page.
x-oneagent-js-injection, x-ruxit-js-agent.

domains.agency: Office of the Brooklyn Borough President (BPBK)
domains.platform: Headless WordPress on WP Engine + Next.js, behind Cloudflare (www.brooklynbp.nyc.gov) — after the office let its former primary domain brooklyn-usa.org lapse (now redirecting offsite to batman-news.com).
domains.tagline: A thin borough office with real but scattered open data, a generic CMS API, and a lapsed domain — the fruit is one templated Borough President API reused across all five boroughs.
domains.netnew: CommunityBoardApplication — apply to serve on a Brooklyn community board (net-new write; today only a web form).
findings: The office LET ITS OWN DOMAIN GO: brooklyn-usa.org now 301-redirects to batman-news.com (WordPress 'Redirection'/'Permalink Manager' plugins on WPX Cloud, bm-*-api migration namespaces). The live office site is www.brooklynbp.nyc.gov — headless WordPress on WP Engine + Next.js, behind Cloudflare.

Contrary to the brief's guess of zero, there ARE 21 NYC Open Data datasets under 'Brooklyn Borough President (BPBK)': ULURP land-use recommendations, a community-board contact list, a dozen 'BP Appointments' tables, and capital/discretionary/tourism award tables.

But the open data is fragmented and uneven: 21 single-purpose Socrata IDs, many thin (3-6 columns), several stale snapshots (Cornerstone Awards 2015-2017, Discretionary Contract Awards 2017-2018). Nothing is an owned, purpose-built BP API.

The one real, live, purpose-shaped read API is generic CMS plumbing: the WordPress REST API (wp/v2/posts, 315 posts) and The Events Calendar (tribe/events/v1/events) — machine-readable but not a Borough-President contract.

There is no write surface for the constituent action that matters most each year — applying to serve on a community board — which today is only an unstructured web form. That is the net-new WRITE object (CommunityBoardApplication).

All five NYC Borough Presidents run near-identical thin offices with the same powers (ULURP advice, board appointments, discretionary funding, community-board coordination) — the fruit is ONE shared, templated Borough President API, not five bespoke ones. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 21 BPBK datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. Real, open, machine-readable — but scattered across 21 single-purpose IDs, several thin/stale.
Live, purpose-shaped read API for events/hearings/board meetings; 6 upcoming events at assessment. The one BP-specific live API surface.
Generic CMS content API — 315 posts (press releases/news), 74+ pages. Machine-readable but not a purpose-built BP contract; only /wp-admin/ is disallowed in robots.txt.

domains.agency: Kings County (Brooklyn) District Attorney's Office
domains.platform: WordPress 6.8.5 (GeneratePress / GP Premium) on Apache; Wordfence, Yoast, MonsterInsights; accidental WordPress REST API (wp/v2)
domains.tagline: A WordPress brochure site with no data, an accidental content API, and no way to send in a tip — one of five identical county DA offices.
domains.netnew: TipSubmission — submit and track a confidential tip (report fraud, an unsafe landlord, a hate incident, trafficking, or information on an open case)
findings: The Brooklyn DA is a content-only domain with no data: a single self-hosted WordPress 6.8.5 brochure site (brooklynda.org, Apache, GeneratePress, Wordfence) — no portal, no application, no data platform.
The only machine-readable surface is the WordPress REST API (/wp-json/wp/v2), an accidental content API that exposes ~911 press releases plus notifications, statements, and bureau/program pages.

There is ZERO NYC Open Data for any DA office — the Socrata Discovery API returns no assets for every Brooklyn/Kings County DA label. The core justice data (caseloads, dispositions, diversions, conviction-review outcomes) is published nowhere as data, only as prose inside press releases.
No inbound machine surface: submitting a tip — the everyday inbound transaction — has only a captcha-gated web form, a phone hotline, or a bureau email.

All five NYC county DA offices share the same functions (bureaus, diversion, conviction review, victim services, press). This is a candidate for ONE shared DA API, built once and adopted five times. Net-new write surface: submit a confidential tip (TipSubmission). See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: The one real machine-readable surface. ~911 press releases (?categories=pr) plus notifications, statements, and bureau/program pages. Accidental — CMS default, raw WordPress fields, not a designed resource model.
ZERO datasets under any Brooklyn/Kings County DA label (verified via Socrata Discovery API). The office publishes no open data.
Only inbound path for a tip/complaint besides phone/email. reCAPTCHA/hCaptcha/Turnstile per Permissions-Policy header. No JSON, no documented submit endpoint.
Bureaus, programs, press releases, brochures, resources. NOT on the nyc.gov shared platform — the office runs its own domain.

domains.agency: NYC Board of Standards & Appeals (BSA)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' (Akamai + nginx + AWS ALB + Dynatrace); records/decisions search is a server-rendered Livesite widget; intake is paper PDF forms — no online application portal
domains.tagline: BSA publishes what it decided but offers no way to file — the intake is still paper PDF forms.
domains.netnew: VarianceApplication — file a zoning variance / special permit / extension / appeal (today a paper PDF-form process)
findings: BSA is a records-forward agency on the shared NYC.gov 'Livesite' chassis (Akamai/nginx/AWS ALB/Dynatrace) with NO online application portal.

Case OUTCOMES are open: 4 NYC Open Data datasets cover every application filed 1998–present with type (BZ variance/special-permit, BZY extension, SOC amendment, Appeal), premises, zoning district, status (Granted/Denied/Withdrawn/Dismissed), and a link to the decision PDF, plus a legacy calendar index back to 1916 and a pre-application-meetings log.
But the INTAKE has no machine-readable surface: filing a variance/appeal is a paper PDF-form process (bz_form.pdf, appeal_form.pdf, bzy_form.pdf, soc_form.pdf) with no online portal and no API.

The records/decisions search is a server-rendered Livesite widget (searchRecords()), not an API; resolutions are unstructured PDFs; the public hearing calendar exists only as HTML/PDF with Zoom links, with no Open Data twin.
Net-new write surface: file a zoning variance/appeal (VarianceApplication) — the core BSA transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 4 BSA datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open BSA API — case outcomes/index only.
server-rendered search widget over decided cases 1998–present (searchRecords(), submit=true&componentID=…); no JSON/OpenAPI.
bz_form.pdf, appeal_form.pdf, bzy_form.pdf, soc_form.pdf + checklists — the filing workflow, on paper. No online portal.
the written resolution per case — a PDF, not structured data.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, AWS ALB, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
referenced applications/zoning-lot map; a DCP surface, not a BSA API.

domains.agency: NYC Commission on Human Rights (CCHR)
domains.platform: NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' platform (Akamai edge, nginx, AWS ALB, Dynatrace + mPulse RUM) — no CCHR-specific application platform and, unlike NYCHA, no vendor CRM.
domains.tagline: CCHR's core transaction — reporting discrimination — is an untyped web form with no API, backed by only three aggregate datasets and a Human Rights Law published as prose and PDF.
domains.netnew: A typed DiscriminationComplaint intake API (report discrimination) — replacing the untyped Report Discrimination HTML form.
findings: CCHR is the thinnest domain yet: NYC Open Data carries only 3 CCHR datasets — Inquiries Received, Office of Mediation cases, and Pre-Complaint Resolutions — and all three are aggregate operational counts, not case data.

The core citizen transaction, reporting discrimination, is a plain server-rendered HTML form on the shared NYC.gov 'Livesite' platform that posts into an opaque Law Enforcement Bureau backend — no API, no OpenAPI, no JSON, no documented POST schema.
The NYC Human Rights Law itself — protected classes and legal enforcement guidance — is published only as prose and PDF; 'is X protected in housing?' has no machine-readable answer.

Individual complaint/respondent/case data is never published (privacy by design); the intake form's typed structure is discarded and re-surfaces only as an annual tally whose columns match the form's own category choices.
Net-new write surface: report discrimination (DiscriminationComplaint) — typing the untyped intake form. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Only 3 CCHR datasets (395v-hkhg, tmha-56pf, 6ayi-8khd); SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one open API — aggregate operational counts only.
The core transaction. Server-rendered HTML <form> (name, category, respondent, incident date, description, up to 3 attachments). No OpenAPI/JSON; posts to an opaque Law Enforcement Bureau backend.
Shared NYC.gov mail/contact-form validation service referenced by the page — not a CCHR complaint API.
The law, legal library, complaint process, trainings/workshops — content only. Akamai edge, nginx, AWS ALB, Dynatrace + mPulse RUM. No content API.

domains.agency: NYC Civilian Complaint Review Board (CCRB)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' shared platform (Akamai/nginx/AWS ALB/Dynatrace) + a Complaint Status Lookup app on apps.nyc.gov; data via NYC Open Data (Socrata) and in-house Data Transparency Initiative dashboards
domains.tagline: CCRB's accountability data is a national transparency model, but it ships as dashboards and CSV with no API, and the complaint it exists to intake is a web form.
domains.netnew: MisconductComplaint — file a police-misconduct complaint against an officer via API (today an online web form only)
findings: CCRB is a police-oversight transparency model whose data is generously open but delivered ONLY as Data Transparency Initiative dashboards and four daily-updated Socrata datasets (complaints, allegations, officers, penalties) — there is no API.

The corpus is a tightly-normalized accountability model keyed on Complaint Id and Tax ID: officer-level Total/Substantiated complaint counts, per-allegation FADO records with CCRB-vs-NYPD dispositions, and the APU-vs-NYPD penalty gap.

The two acts a member of the public performs — filing a misconduct complaint (.../file-online) and checking its status (apps.nyc.gov/ccrb-status-lookup) — are disconnected JavaScript screens with no machine-readable contract.
Net-new write surface: file a MisconductComplaint against an officer — the reason the agency exists — has no API and no Open Data twin. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.
The gap is delivery, not openness: the record is model-grade, but it is not a queryable, agent-native API, and intake is not connected to it.

apis_observed.note: 4 CCRB datasets (2fir-qns4, 6xgr-kwjq, 2mby-ccnw, keep-pkmh); SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints, updated daily and automated. The read side of the CCRB Complaints Database — the one real, open CCRB API.
File a misconduct complaint online; server-rendered JavaScript page, no JSON/OpenAPI. The net-new write gap.
Check the status of a filed complaint; standalone screen disconnected from intake, no machine-readable contract.

About, complaint process, policy, and the Data Transparency Initiative dashboards (complaints/allegations/members-of-service/victims/feedback). Akamai edge, nginx, AWS ALB, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
Alternate complaint channel referenced from CCRB's file-a-complaint pages.

domains.agency: NYC Campaign Finance Board (CFB)
domains.platform: Microsoft IIS 8.5 / ASP.NET MVC 5.2 + AngularJS 1.5 (nyccfb.info + FTMSearchWebAPI); Umbraco CMS / ASP.NET on Azure (nycvotes.org); C-SMART / IEDS / C-Access ASP.NET applications
domains.tagline: CFB already publishes its campaign-finance data every way except as a documented, owned, agent-native API.
domains.netnew: DisclosureFiling — a filer submits a disclosure statement (today only via the login-walled C-SMART disclosure application)
findings: CFB is the transparency leader among NYC agencies and is *almost* API-first already: it runs on its own domain (nyccfb.info, not nyc.gov), publishes 16 NYC Open Data datasets, ships a bulk Data Library of per-cycle CSVs back to 2001, and its Follow the Money search is a single-page app backed by a REAL, working JSON Web API (nyccfb.info/FTMSearchWebAPI).
But that API is undocumented, backend-only, and unowned as a product — no OpenAPI, no developer docs, no stable public contract, no agent-native surface. It is a de-facto API, not a published one.

The reference/analysis data is exceptionally rich: contributions with full intermediary/bundler detail (rjkp-yttg, 54 columns), expenditures (qxzj-vkn2), public matching-funds payments (u69g-mvrb), and per-candidate financial analysis (m3tj-a2pb) — in parallel citywide and off-year/special-election streams.

The write layer is locked. Filing a disclosure statement happens only inside C-SMART, the CFB's login-walled web-based disclosure application; there is no submission API and no Open Data twin (only Late/Missing Disclosure Statements, 2ujk-6z7u, exposes filing status).
Net-new write surface: submit a disclosure statement (DisclosureFiling) — the one campaign-finance transaction with no machine-readable contract today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.type: backend JSON Web API (ASP.NET Web API)
apis_observed.note: Powers the Follow the Money AngularJS search. Confirmed live JSON from /api/Common/GetElectionCycle, plus /api/Common/* reference lists and /api/AutoComplete/* typeaheads for candidates, contributors, payees, intermediaries, and independent spenders. Real API, no OpenAPI/docs — the core finding.
16 CFB datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. Contributions, expenditures, intermediaries, public funds, financial analysis, enforcement, voter analysis.
56 flat CSV files (contribution, expenditure, payment, intermediary, analysis) per election cycle from 2001 through 2025. Bulk twin of the Open Data / FTM data.

Web-based application campaigns use to disclose (submit) contributions/expenditures — i.e. file. The net-new write surface; no JSON/OpenAPI. Was showing an 'unavailable' maintenance notice at crawl time.
Independent Expenditure Disclosure System — a separate login-walled filing app for outside spenders.
Access portal referenced from the FTM search header.
The public voter-engagement brand; Umbraco CMS / ASP.NET on Azure App Service. contribute.nycvotes.org and nycvotes.turbovote.org are separate services. No public content API observed.
appInsights telemetry injected on the FTM search page.

domains.agency: New York City Office of the City Clerk (OCC)
domains.platform: cityclerk.nyc.gov on the NYC.gov Content API (AWS ALB + Akamai + nginx + Dynatrace, jQuery/Bootstrap, WebTrends); Project Cupid marriage system on the rented Unqork no-code platform (Angular SPA); e-Lobbyist Java filing app behind SAP CDC / Gigya SSO; Lobbyist Search JS SPA over a .do Java backend
domains.tagline: The City Clerk rents the machinery of the city's two most personal civic transactions — getting married and registering to lobby — and owns no API contract over either.
domains.netnew: MarriageLicenseApplication — a couple applies for a NYC marriage license (today only via Project Cupid's Unqork screens)
findings: The City Clerk runs two bureaus on rented, agent-invisible platforms: the Marriage Bureau's Project Cupid is a no-code application on the rented Unqork platform, and the Lobbying Bureau's e-Lobbyist is a login-walled Java app behind rented SAP CDC / Gigya SSO.

The flagship citizen transaction — applying for a marriage license — has NO API and NO Open Data twin at all. It lives only inside Project Cupid's Unqork screens; NYC's most personal civic transaction is outsourced to a no-code vendor.

The Lobbying Bureau is the more open half: registration and periodic filing happen in e-Lobbyist, but the reported data IS published — two NYC Open Data datasets (City Clerk eLobbyist Data fmf3-knd8, 28c; Lobbyists' Fundraising & Political Consulting Reports 7arw-dbem, 24c) plus a public Lobbyist Search.
Marriage records are NOT the City Clerk's on Open Data — only DORIS publishes historical vital-records indexes (j62e-7maa, d8dr-nyhw). The live marriage side is entirely closed.

The informational site was quietly modernized onto the NYC.gov Content API (apps.nyc.gov/content-api/v1) — a real JSON backend serving content and navigation — but it is undocumented, shared, and read-only.
Net-new write surface: apply for a marriage license (MarriageLicenseApplication) — the single most common Marriage Bureau transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Real JSON backend that drives cityclerk.nyc.gov (content + navigation). Read-only, undocumented, shared across NYC.gov agency sites. Content only — no transactions.

Online marriage-license application, appointment scheduling, and ceremony booking. Built on the rented Unqork no-code platform (polyfill.unqork.io, /fbu/ uapi, Angular SPA). No documented API, no OpenAPI, no Open Data output. The net-new write surface (MarriageLicenseApplication).
e-Lobbyist registration and periodic filing. Java servlet app (JSESSIONID, .do) fronted by SAP CDC / Gigya SAML SSO (fidm.us1.gigya.com). No public API; reported data is published to Open Data.
Lobbyist Search public database — JS SPA (webpack bundle) over a .do Java backend. Public but no documented/OpenAPI contract.
2 City Clerk datasets (lobbyist filing + fundraising reports), each a SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoint. The only open, machine-readable City Clerk API — and only for the lobbying side.
City Clerk Forms Online (officiant registration, records requests). Server-rendered Java forms app; no API.

domains.agency: NYC Conflicts of Interest Board (COIB)
domains.platform: NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' chassis (Akamai + nginx + Dynatrace) for the informational site, plus a separate login-gated Annual Financial Disclosure electronic filing website
domains.tagline: COIB publishes its enforcement and transparency data openly, but the core compliance act — filing an annual financial disclosure — is trapped behind a login-only filing website with no API.
domains.netnew: FinancialDisclosureFiling — a public servant files/attests an annual financial disclosure report, today locked inside the login-only COIB filing website with no API
findings: COIB is a transparency-out / attestation-in agency: its enforcement and transparency OUTPUTS are genuinely open (8 NYC Open Data datasets), but its core compliance INPUT — the annual financial disclosure filing — is locked in a login-gated filing website with no API.

The 8 open datasets cover enforcement dispositions/fines (Enforcement Fines, the most-viewed asset), donations to agencies and to elected-official-affiliated non-profits, the monthly Policymakers List, and legal defense trust donations/refunds/expenditures.

The Annual Disclosure system is the real transaction layer: 'file electronically through COIB's filing website' with a 4-week filing window each spring, but it is login-only, produces no Open Data twin, and elected officials' reports surface only as PDFs on request.
Advisory opinions (Charter Chapter 68 interpretations) and ethics-training records are published only as PDFs / behind the training site — reference corpora with no machine-readable index.

Net-new write surface: file an annual financial disclosure report (FinancialDisclosureFiling) — the single most consequential COIB resident-of-government transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 8 COIB datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open COIB API — enforcement dispositions, donations, policymakers, and legal defense trusts.

content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API. Advisory opinions and annual reports are PDFs.
Google Maps embed on the informational site.
Akamai mPulse real-user-monitoring beacon.

domains.agency: City University of New York (CUNY)
domains.platform: WordPress Multisite (nginx) + Oracle PeopleSoft Campus Solutions (CUNYfirst)
domains.tagline: One owned API to federate ~25 campuses' reference data and unlock the CUNYfirst application it now hides.
domains.netnew: AdmissionsApplication (submit a CUNY application)
findings: CUNY is a FEDERATED system: ~25 colleges and schools, each with its own website, all on a shared WordPress Multisite (cuny.edu, nginx) and a single shared Oracle PeopleSoft Campus Solutions student information system branded CUNYfirst.

CUNY is a New York STATE public benefit corporation, not a NYC agency — so unlike NYCHA, DOE, or the Council it publishes NOTHING under a CUNY agency label on NYC Open Data. The label returns exactly one asset, and it is an external link, not a dataset.

Its reference data is scattered: campus directories and program search are HTML on cuny.edu; enrollment/demographics are PDF/Excel data books from the Office of Institutional Research and Assessment (OIRA); state enrollment tables live on data.ny.gov. None is a clean API.

The transactional system of record — applications, courses, enrollment, financial aid — is CUNYfirst (PeopleSoft), reachable only through login-optional guest self-service screens (/psc/...GBL). No public API, no OpenAPI, no JSON.

Net-new write surface: submit a CUNY admissions application (AdmissionsApplication) — the single most common prospective-student transaction, covering multiple campuses in one application, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: CUNYfirst — admissions (CU_E1385_CNSLR_FL), course catalog browse (SSS_BROWSE_CATLG), enrollment, financial aid. Server-rendered PeopleSoft PIA (/psc/, .GBL, PT_LANDINGPAGE), JavaScript-only, no JSON/OpenAPI. Behind an F5 BIG-IP.
Central site + ~25 federated college subsites. WPBakery + Slider Revolution. wp-json responds but is content-only; no product/reference API.
'Explore Programs' degree-program finder; no documented backing API exposed to consumers.
CUNY Academic Works — faculty/research output; OAI-PMH harvest per college, no unified REST API.
Effectively ZERO CUNY presence: the CUNY agency label returns one external-link asset; one CUNY-subject budget dataset (wusu-mzmq) is tagged to OMB.
State-level SUNY/CUNY enrollment tables live on the STATE portal, not the city's — reflecting CUNY's status as a state public benefit corporation.

domains.agency: New York City Department of Citywide Administrative Services (DCAS)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Livesite (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) content + a fragmented aNNN vendor app layer: City Jobs (ASP.NET Core/Azure), Employee Self-Service (PeopleSoft/NYCAPS), OASys, and the CityStore (Shopify)
domains.tagline: DCAS publishes the civil-service machine as open data but hides every transaction behind a rented vendor portal.
domains.netnew: ExamRegistration — register for a civil-service exam (alt: JobApplication)
findings: DCAS is a fragmented domain: an informational NYC.gov site (Akamai/nginx/Livesite/Dynatrace) plus a sprawl of separate aNNN vendor applications — City Jobs (cityjobs.nyc.gov, ASP.NET Core on Azure), Employee Self-Service (a127-ess.nyc.gov, PeopleSoft/NYCAPS), the exam Online Application System (OASys), and the CityStore (a856-citystore.nyc.gov, Shopify) — none sharing an API.

The civil-service REFERENCE data is broadly open: 32 NYC Open Data datasets covering job postings (Jobs NYC Postings, 30 columns), civil-service titles, active/terminated/certified eligible lists, the annual exam schedule, DCAS-managed public buildings and their energy, and the municipal fleet.

But the citizen TRANSACTION layer has no machine-readable surface: you can read the exam schedule as open data, yet there is no API to actually register for an exam or apply to a posting — those live only inside OASys and the City Jobs portal, behind a login and a fee.

The most-viewed DCAS datasets are the Civil Service List (Active) (3.5M views) and Civil Service List Certification (1.1M views) — the eligible-list machine is DCAS's signature, published as static snapshots with no query API of its own beyond Socrata.

Net-new write surface: register for a civil-service exam (ExamRegistration) — the DCAS-distinctive citizen transaction, with no API today. An alternative net-new write is JobApplication (apply to a Jobs NYC posting). See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 32 DCAS datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DCAS API — civil-service reference, buildings, and fleet data only.

City Jobs external portal + OASys online application; Kestrel server, x-ms-routing-name (Azure App Service), AspNetCore.Antiforgery. Job applications and exam registrations happen here; no JSON/OpenAPI.
NYC Automated Personnel System employee self-service; PeopleSoft (psp/ path, page title). Legacy vendor HR app, no public API.

apis_observed.public: public UI; Shopify API not city-owned
apis_observed.note: The official CityStore runs on Shopify (Cloudflare edge, _shopify_* cookies). Storefront/Admin APIs exist but are vendor-tenant, not a DCAS contract.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.

domains.agency: New York City Department of Cultural Affairs (DCLA)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' v22 info site (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) + a separate Salesforce Experience Cloud grants portal (dclagms.nyc.gov, backed by culturalaffairsnyc.my.salesforce.com); 9 Socrata datasets
domains.tagline: DCLA publishes who it funds and for how much, but the grant application that produces those outcomes is locked in a Salesforce portal with no API.
domains.netnew: Apply for a Cultural Development Fund grant (GrantApplication) — the core transaction with no API, locked in the Salesforce Grants Management System
findings: DCLA is a grantmaker that publishes outcomes but not the application: 9 NYC Open Data datasets cover program and capital grant awards, Cultural Institutions Group support, a geocoded cultural-organization directory, completed Percent for Art commissions, and Materials for the Arts donation summaries.

The core transaction — applying for a Cultural Development Fund grant — lives only in the Grants Management System (dclagms.nyc.gov), a Salesforce Experience Cloud community reached by login, with no public API and no Open Data twin.
No owned contract binds an organization to its funding; the funding datasets key on organization name and application # with no stable id, so an agent must stitch names across 9 Socrata IDs by hand.
Materials for the Arts data is published only in aggregate by year and category; no individual donor is ever exposed.
Net-new write surface: apply for a grant (GrantApplication) — the most important interaction between DCLA and its constituency, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 9 DCLA datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DCLA API — funding-outcome and directory data only.

domains.agency: Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' info site (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) + a separate Java/Spring NYC Business licensing portal (nyc-business.nyc.gov) + CityPay (CityBase) payments; 37 Socrata datasets
domains.tagline: DCWP already publishes its entire regulated-business lifecycle openly; the work is binding it into one owned contract and adding the two citizen writes.
domains.netnew: File a consumer complaint (ConsumerComplaint) and apply for a business license (LicenseApplication) — the two citizen writes with no API
findings: DCWP is the most-open domain in this project: 37 NYC Open Data datasets publish the entire regulated-business lifecycle — license applications, issued licenses (147k views), inspections, charges, consumer complaints, revocations, and Office of Labor Policy & Standards worker-protection matters.

The whole lifecycle is joinable on `Business Unique ID` and `License Number`, but no single owned API binds those 37 datasets into one coherent resource model — a consumer or agent must learn 37 Socrata IDs.

The two things a person actually DOES have no API: applying for a business license lives only in the Java NYC Business portal (nyc-business.nyc.gov), and filing a consumer complaint lives only in 311 or a DCWP web form.
Worker-protection data (OLPS inquiries and enforcement matters) is published only in aggregate by topic/industry/geography — never an individual worker.

Net-new write surface: file a consumer complaint (ConsumerComplaint) and apply for a license (LicenseApplication) — both have an Open Data READ twin but no write API. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 37 DCWP datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DCWP API — the full licensing/enforcement lifecycle as reference data.
Apply for and manage DCWP licenses. `SESSION` cookie, Akamai edge, Dynatrace RUM; no JSON/OpenAPI surface.
Pay DCWP fines/fees; CityBase-hosted checkout, returns 403 to non-browser clients.
The intake path for consumer complaints against businesses; no public write API to DCWP.
Content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
`server-timing: ak_p` across the info site and NYC Business portal.
`x-oneagent-js-injection` real-user monitoring.

domains.agency: New York City Department of Design and Construction (DDC)
domains.platform: NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' platform (Akamai + nginx + Dynatrace + mPulse + AWS ALB) for the informational site; the transaction layer is entirely citywide systems DDC does not own — PASSPort (MOCS), City Record, Checkbook NYC (Comptroller), plus DDC's own API-less DDC Anywhere portal.
domains.tagline: A vendor-facing agency whose own data is thin and historical and whose transactions all run on citywide systems it doesn't own.
domains.netnew: VendorPrequalification — a firm submits a prequalification / expression of interest (optionally against a solicitation, with an optional City Record notice opt-in). A B2G write; DDC is vendor-facing, so there is no citizen write in this domain.
findings: DDC is a vendor-facing, business-to-government agency — the City's primary capital construction project manager (550+ active projects, $34B+ portfolio) that builds for other agencies. There is no citizen and no citizen transaction anywhere in the domain.

DDC exposes NO API of its own. Its only machine-readable data is 4 NYC Open Data datasets (Socrata SODA), three of them frozen '(Historical)' snapshots of active projects, plus a 4-column directory of awarded contracts.

Every transaction is outsourced to citywide systems DDC does not own: solicitations and vendor prequalification run in PASSPort (managed by MOCS), notices go to the City Record, and contract records live on Checkbook NYC (Comptroller). The one DDC-owned vendor portal (DDC Anywhere) has no API.
The richer live capital data (Capital Commitment Plan / Capital Project Detail Data) is published under OMB/Comptroller labels, not DDC — so even DDC's own portfolio is not fully DDC-published.

Net-new write surface: submit a vendor prequalification / expression of interest (VendorPrequalification) — a B2G transaction that today lives only inside citywide PASSPort. NOTE: there is no citizen write for this agency. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Only 4 DDC datasets: Active Projects + two '(Historical)' variants and the awarded-contracts directory. SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DDC data surface — thin and mostly historical.
Where ALL DDC solicitations are released and responded to; vendor registration + prequalification. Not DDC-owned; no OpenAPI/JSON.
Solicitation and award notices; vendors register for email alerts.
Public record of DDC/City contracts — but the API belongs to the Comptroller, not DDC.
The one DDC-owned vendor system — design/construction process for contract holders. No machine-readable surface observed.
Content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace + mPulse RUM, AWS ALB. No content API.

domains.agency: New York City Department of Environmental Protection (DEP)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Livesite (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) + uMAX CIS on ASP.NET + Azure AD B2C
domains.tagline: The water data is wide open; the water transactions are locked in a billing portal and dumped into 311.
domains.netnew: WaterServiceRequest
findings: DEP is a split, data-rich but transaction-poor domain: an informational NYC.gov site (Akamai/nginx/Livesite/Dynatrace) plus a customer 'My DEP Account' portal that is a uMAX utility CIS on ASP.NET + Azure AD B2C (a826-umax.dep.nyc.gov) with no API.

The reference/telemetry data is unusually OPEN: 57 NYC Open Data datasets covering water consumption (ia2d-e54m, DEP's most-viewed), reservoir levels (zkky-n5j3), harbor/drinking/watershed/lead-copper water quality, green infrastructure, hydrants & catch basins, and water/air/asbestos permits.

But that corpus is sprawling and inconsistently typed — Harbor Water Quality is 100 free-text columns, reservoir levels are cryptic SCADA tags, and there are ~a dozen near-duplicate Watershed Water Quality tables. Open does not mean usable.

The customer SERVICE layer is split and locked: water-bill payment and account management live only inside the login-walled uMAX portal, while street conditions (water-main break, no water, sewer backup, clogged catch basin, hydrant) are funneled into generic NYC311 — neither is a DEP-owned API.

Net-new write surface: report a water/sewer problem (WaterServiceRequest) — the most urgent resident transaction, with no single machine-readable contract today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 57 DEP datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The real, open DEP API — reference/telemetry data only, and sprawling/inconsistently typed.
water/sewer bill payment + account management; ASP.NET / ASP.NET Core SPA behind Azure AD B2C (umaxazprodb2c.b2clogin.com), on Azure App Service. Public /quickpay page but no JSON/OpenAPI.
where DEP street conditions (main break, no water, sewer backup, catch basin, hydrant, water quality) are actually reported — generic 311, not a DEP-owned intake.
DEP's completed work orders / service requests — closest open twin to the locked service layer, but read-only and after the fact.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.

domains.agency: NYC Department for the Aging (DFTA / NYC Aging)
domains.platform: NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' platform (Akamai + nginx + Dynatrace) — no separate vendor system; the service layer is a phone-based Aging Connect information-and-referral contact center.
domains.tagline: The provider network is open data; the act of connecting an older adult to a service is a phone call.
domains.netnew: ServiceReferral — connect an older adult to DFTA services (case management, meals, benefits, caregiver, elder abuse, center enrollment) with an urgent path to Aging Connect / Adult Protective Services.
findings: DFTA (NYC Aging) is a connective, network-funding agency: it rarely delivers services itself, it funds ~1,000 community providers — so its reference data is a directory of providers, sites, senior-center operations, activities, and contract service units.

The provider/program reference data is well OPEN: 11 NYC Open Data datasets covering all contracted providers (38 columns), publicly open sites, older adult center (senior center) Local Law 140 operations, activities, budgeted/reported service units, expenditures, and aggregate participation.

But the resident SERVICE layer — connecting an older adult to case management, home-delivered meals, benefits, caregiver support, elder-abuse help, or senior center enrollment — has no machine-readable surface at all: intake happens only by calling Aging Connect (212-AGING-NYC), calling 311, or walking into a center.
Client/participant data is published only in aggregate (Number of Participants, unduplicated clients); DFTA never exposes an individual older adult record to Open Data.
Net-new write surface: make a service referral (ServiceReferral) — connect an older adult to services — the core resident transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 11 DFTA datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DFTA API — provider/program reference data only.

apis_observed.public: phone / walk-in / web form, no API
apis_observed.note: The intake and referral layer — case management, home-delivered meals, benefits, caregiver, elder abuse, center enrollment. No JSON/OpenAPI; a contact center, not a system with a machine-readable surface.

domains.agency: NYC Department of Homeless Services (DHS)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' shared chassis (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace); no service portal — transactions route through NYC311
domains.tagline: DHS publishes a rich, open picture of homelessness but gives New Yorkers no API to act on it.
domains.netnew: OutreachRequest — request street outreach for a person experiencing homelessness (today only via NYC311)
findings: DHS is an observational-data agency: its informational site is the shared NYC.gov 'Livesite' chassis (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) and it ships no application of its own — every transaction happens on NYC311, 911, or at an in-person intake center.

The reference data is unusually OPEN: 23 NYC Open Data datasets led by the flagship DHS Daily Report (the daily shelter census, DHS's single most-viewed asset at 87k views), plus drop-in centers, DHS contacts/intake centers, buildings and individual census, the Shelter Repair Scorecard (55 columns), and years of unsheltered street-count history.

But there is NO service layer to expose — applying for shelter is in-person and reporting a person on the street for outreach is a NYC311 'Homeless Person Assistance' call; neither has a machine-readable contract, and DHS does not even own the 311 channel that stands in for one.
People are published only in aggregate (shelter census and street counts); DHS never exposes an individual record to Open Data.

Net-new write surface: request street outreach (OutreachRequest) — reporting a person experiencing unsheltered homelessness so an outreach team responds, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.
