domains.agency: New York City Department of Correction (DOC)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' (Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM) informational site + a legacy Apache MyFaces (JSF/Java) Inmate Lookup Service ('P.I.C. Lookup', a073-ils-web.nyc.gov) with no API; Socrata SODA open data
domains.tagline: DOC over-publishes accountability data and even runs a live custody lookup, yet the lookup is a JSF screen with no API and the human transactions — visiting someone, filing a complaint — have no digital contract at all.
domains.netnew: VisitScheduling — schedule a visit with a person in custody (no API today; arranged by phone, in person, or vendor portal). Complaint / records request is a second net-new write.
findings: DOC is transparency-heavy but transaction-poor: 15 NYC Open Data datasets publish the daily in-custody population, admissions/discharges, deaths, slashings/stabbings, fights, assaults on staff, staff injuries, emergency lock-ins, and Local Law 33/85 security and visitation indicators.

It also runs a LIVE real-time lookup — the Inmate Lookup Service / 'P.I.C. Lookup' at a073-ils-web.nyc.gov/inmatelookup — but that lookup is a legacy Apache MyFaces (JavaServer Faces) web application with no API, no OpenAPI, and no JSON; it is reachable only by a human in a browser.

The two things the public actually needs to DO — schedule a visit with a person in custody, and file a complaint or records/FOIL request — have no machine-readable surface at all; they are handled by phone, in person, mail, or vendor/OpenRecords portals.

Individual-level custody data exists in two disconnected forms: a daily anonymized snapshot on Open Data (INMATEID only) and a live named lookup keyed on NYSID / Book & Case number — neither is an API.
Net-new write surface: schedule a visit (VisitScheduling); a second net-new write is file a complaint / records request. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 15 DOC datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DOC API — accountability/statistics data only, no live lookup.
Real-time person-in-custody search by NYSID / Book & Case / name; server-rendered JavaServer Faces (javax.faces.ViewState, myfaces), WebSphere-style JSESSIONID, JavaScript-only, no JSON/OpenAPI.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.

domains.agency: New York City Department of Finance (DOF)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' informational chassis (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) plus a fleet of aging, siloed a836-*.nyc.gov apps — ACRIS (ASP.NET/IIS, 2013-era), CityPay (PayPal/Braintree form), and the Property Tax System.
domains.tagline: DOF publishes more open data than any NYC agency assessed, yet every citizen transaction lives behind a fleet of aging, API-less a836 legacy apps.
domains.netnew: ParkingTicketPayment — pay a parking/camera violation via API instead of the CityPay-only web form.
findings: DOF has the widest open-data footprint of any NYC agency assessed so far — 145 NYC Open Data datasets covering property valuation & assessment, exemptions/abatements, tax-charge balances, the full ACRIS document register, and every parking/camera violation.

But its citizen transactions live across a fleet of aging, siloed a836-*.nyc.gov apps — ACRIS (a 2013-era ASP.NET/IIS document-search app), CityPay (a PayPal/Braintree web form), and the Property Tax System — none of which exposes an API, OpenAPI, or JSON contract.

This is the fifth distinct pattern: not data-as-HTML (Parks), not rented search (DOE), not fragmented-but-real APIs (Council), not one locked vendor CRM (NYCHA) — but a spread of in-house legacy application silos sitting on top of an unusually open data layer.

The reference model is coherent and joinable on the Borough-Block-Lot (BBL): valuation, exemptions, tax charges, and ACRIS legals all key on it. Publishing that as one owned resource model spares consumers 145 Socrata IDs and three legacy screens.

Net-new write surface: pay a parking/camera violation (ParkingTicketPayment) — the balance is already published (Open Parking and Camera Violations, nc67-uf89), but the act of paying has no API; it lives only in CityPay. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 145 DOF datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DOF API — valuation, exemptions, tax charges, ACRIS register, parking violations. Reference/snapshot data only.

ACRIS — deeds, mortgages, parties, legals. iso-8859-1 meta-refresh framesets to /CP/, X-Powered-By: ASP.NET, Last-Modified 2013. Server-rendered; no JSON/OpenAPI. Open Data twin exists (bnx9-e6tj et al.) but the live app is API-less.
Pay parking tickets, property taxes, and other charges. CSP allowlists js.braintreegateway.com and *.paypal.com — a PayPal/Braintree-backed form. No public payment API. The net-new write surface.
Property Tax System account access; Akamai-fronted, session-cookie gated (mspwvw-* cookie). Server-rendered; no API.
Content only — About, property, benefits, how-to. Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM, livesite-version: 22. No content API.

domains.agency: New York City Department of Investigation (DOI)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' shared chassis (Akamai + nginx + Livesite v22 + Dynatrace) for the informational site; public reports are static PDFs under /assets/doi/reports/pdf/. The complaint-intake service layer is Kaseware, a third-party case-management SaaS (app.kaseware.us, Cloudflare-fronted) — a public web form with no API.
domains.tagline: The city's Inspector General publishes its investigations only as PDFs and takes its complaints through a third-party vendor form.
domains.netnew: CorruptionComplaint — file and track a fraud/waste/corruption tip (with anonymous filing and Whistleblower-Law protection), the transaction that starts every DOI investigation and today lives only in a vendor Kaseware form.
findings: DOI is New York City's Inspector General, but its core work product — public investigation reports and reform recommendations — is published ONLY as PDFs (nyc.gov/assets/doi/reports/pdf/<year>/), with no structured Open Data twin and no content API.

DOI's four Open Data datasets are not its investigations: they are dominated by City Marshal oversight — executed Evictions (6z8x-wfk4, ~237k views, DOI's most-viewed asset) and City Marshals Revenue — plus the Policy & Procedure Recommendations tracker and monthly performance indicators.

The single most important inbound transaction — reporting fraud, waste, or corruption — runs through a THIRD-PARTY vendor form: Kaseware (app.kaseware.us), a case-management SaaS, with phone/fax/mail as fallbacks. There is no API.
The one genuinely structured investigative-output twin is the PPR Portal (jstn-jaut): DOI's recommendations to agencies plus acceptance and implementation status.
Net-new write surface: file a corruption complaint (CorruptionComplaint) — the tip that starts every investigation, today locked in the Kaseware portal. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 4 DOI datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. Mostly City Marshal oversight (evictions, revenue) plus PPR recommendations and performance indicators — not the investigations themselves.
The 'Submit Report Online' corruption-complaint form. Third-party Kaseware Portal SPA; no JSON/OpenAPI surface. This is the net-new write gap.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Livesite v22, Dynatrace RUM. Public reports served as static PDFs under /assets/doi/reports/pdf/. No content API.
referenced as an alternate reporting channel for some complaint types.

domains.agency: NYC Department of Records & Information Services (DORIS)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Livesite site + Squarespace (archives.nyc) + Preservica DAMS (formerly LUNA Imaging) + Akamai vital-records portal + OpenRecords; Socrata for the indexes
domains.tagline: DORIS publishes rich open indexes to its archives, vital records, and publications, but the records themselves sit in a vendor DAMS and must be ordered by hand.
domains.netnew: RecordsRequest — request/retrieve a record: order a copy of a historical vital record, an archival item/photo, a library publication, or file a FOIL request
findings: DORIS is a many-doored domain: an informational NYC.gov 'Livesite' site (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace), a separate Squarespace collections site (archives.nyc), a vendor DAMS for the archive (Preservica, formerly LUNA Imaging), an Akamai-fronted Historical Vital Records ordering portal, and the citywide OpenRecords FOIL system.

The INDEXES are wide open: 13 NYC Open Data datasets index the historical vital records (birth/death/marriage), the Municipal Archives' digital objects and finding aids, the City Hall Library catalog (the most-viewed DORIS dataset), government publications and required reports, and honorary street names.

But the OBJECTS are locked in a vendor DAMS. Preservica ships a real REST content API (/api/content/search returns JSON) yet it is token-gated for the public catalog — no OpenAPI, no open/bulk access, no agent contract; the scans/images never reach Open Data.

RETRIEVAL has no machine-readable surface at all. Ordering a certified vital-record copy, requesting an archival reproduction, or filing a FOIL request each lives in a separate portal, form, or email — none has an API.

Net-new write surface: request a record (RecordsRequest) — order/retrieve a copy of a vital record, an archival item, or a FOIL request — the thing researchers actually want, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 13 DORIS datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one fully open DORIS API — indexes/finding aids/catalogs only, never the object itself.

Real REST/JSON API holding the digitized objects; GET /api/content/search returns application/json but 401 not.authenticated no.token.header. No published OpenAPI, no open/bulk access, no agent contract.
Prior Municipal Archives online image platform with a documented servlet/JSON API; connection refused at crawl — being superseded by Preservica.
Search the pre-1949 indexes and order certified/uncertified copies; returns 403 to a bare crawler; no JSON/OpenAPI. The retrieval step for vital records.
File/track FOIL requests; a read-only status rollup is on Open Data (kegn-anvq), but the submit workflow has no public API.
Front door to the online archive; content only, no content API.
Content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.

domains.agency: NYC Department of Veterans' Services (DVS)
domains.platform: NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' platform (Akamai + nginx + Dynatrace) for the info site; the care-coordination service layer is VetConnectNYC, a third-party vendor portal on Combined Arms' 'Military Resource Portal' (Next.js on CloudFront) — a web form, no API.
domains.tagline: DVS publishes even its de-identified service data as open data, but the veteran's actual referral runs through a third-party vendor form with no API.
domains.netnew: ServiceReferral — make a VetConnectNYC care-coordination referral connecting a veteran to services (housing, benefits, VA claims, health, employment, education, food, legal), routed to DVS Care Coordinators with an urgent path to a Veteran Resource Center.
findings: DVS is a connective agency with a vendor service layer: it points veterans to a coordinated-care network via VetConnectNYC, which runs on the third-party Combined Arms 'Military Resource Portal' (nyc.veteranportal.combinedarms.us) — no API; DVS Care Coordinators process requests manually within 3-5 business days.

DVS is unusually open: 7 NYC Open Data datasets cover not only reference directories (DVS Resource Map, NYC Veteran Owned Businesses) but de-identified SERVICE data — assistance requests (with a 'VetConnectNYC (Y/N)' flag), cases, client demographics, and historical request processing.

But the live referral is locked: the thing a veteran actually does — submit a VetConnectNYC request and get connected to housing, benefits, VA claims, health, employment, food, or legal help — has no machine-readable contract at all; it lives only in the Combined Arms web form.
Veteran/client data is published only de-identified/aggregate (DVS Clients); DVS never exposes an identifiable veteran record to Open Data.
Vendor correction: VetConnectNYC is Combined Arms, NOT Unite Us (the assignment's tentative guess). See tech-stack.md.
Net-new write surface: make a VetConnectNYC referral (ServiceReferral) — connect a veteran to services — the core resident transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 7 DVS datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DVS API — and unusually, it reaches into the de-identified service layer (assistance requests, cases, clients), not just reference data.

apis_observed.owner: Combined Arms (vendor); DVS Care Coordinators work the queue
apis_observed.note: The transactional layer — a veteran submits the VetConnectNYC Request Form to be connected to services. 'CA - Military Resource Portal', Next.js on CloudFront. No JSON/OpenAPI; requests processed manually within 3-5 business days.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
redirects to www.combinedarms.us; the coordinated-care network behind VetConnectNYC.

domains.agency: NYC Department of Youth and Community Development (DYCD)
domains.platform: Informational site on the NYC.gov 'Livesite' chassis (nginx/Akamai/Dynatrace); the DYCD Connect / DiscoverDYCD apps on Microsoft-IIS + Angular with a private internal /api/
domains.tagline: DYCD already built a public program finder and publishes its whole supply chain as open data — it just never gave either an API, or a way to apply.
domains.netnew: ProgramApplication — apply to a program (e.g. an SYEP application)
findings: DYCD is a split domain: an informational NYC.gov site (nginx/Livesite/Akamai/Dynatrace) plus the DYCD Connect ecosystem — dycdconnect.nyc and the DiscoverDYCD program finder (discoverdycd.dycdconnect.nyc) — an Angular SPA on Microsoft-IIS with a private internal /api/ backend.

DYCD is a funder/intermediary, not a direct service provider: it contracts hundreds of community-based organizations (providers) to run programs (SYEP, COMPASS/SONYC, Beacon, Cornerstone) at physical program sites, and the open data documents that supply chain.

The supply-side data is well published: 15 NYC Open Data datasets covering program sites (ebkm-iyma, 34 columns with a full geography spine, slots, and participants), contracts, contractors/providers, Neighborhood Development Areas, and aggregate participant demographics (k9kq-67vm, 52 columns).

But the program finder is an app, not an API: DYCD already built DiscoverDYCD to help the public find programs, yet its backend /api/ is undocumented and private, and there is no clean Open Data catalog of the program offerings themselves.

Net-new write surface: apply to a program (ProgramApplication) — e.g. an SYEP application — the transaction a young person actually needs, with no public API today; it lives only in DYCD's seasonal online application flow. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 15 DYCD datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DYCD API — supply-side/reference data only (program sites, contracts, providers, NDAs, aggregate demographics).
The DiscoverDYCD program finder is a hashed-bundle Angular app that calls a private /api/ backend (returns 404 to public probes). Uses Google Maps + Places. No public docs, no OpenAPI.
The DYCD Connect ecosystem where providers manage contracts and where program application/enrollment flows live. No public API.
content only; nginx origin behind Akamai edge, Dynatrace RUM, AWS ALB. No content API.
DiscoverDYCD embeds Google Maps JS + Places for site location/search.

domains.agency: New York City Economic Development Corporation (EDC)
domains.platform: Drupal behind Cloudflare (bot-challenged); NYC Ferry on Cloudflare + CloudFront
domains.tagline: EDC publishes ferry rides and a company map, but keeps its real estate, projects, and RFPs off the machine-readable web entirely.
domains.netnew: RFPResponse — register interest in / submit a proposal to an EDC solicitation (RFP/RFEI)
findings: EDC is a public benefit corporation, not a mayoral agency — and its data footprint reflects that: only 5 NYC Open Data assets carry the 'Economic Development Corporation (EDC)' label, and they are peripheral (a marketing company map, NYC Ferry ridership, and three WiredNYC broadband tables).

EDC's actual business is invisible to machines. Its ~60M sq ft real-estate portfolio, its development/capital projects, and its solicitations (RFPs/RFEIs) — the core of what EDC does — have NO Open Data twin and NO API; they exist only as Drupal pages on edc.nyc behind a Cloudflare bot challenge.

The one genuinely operational open dataset is NYC Ferry Ridership (t5n6-gx8c); the rest are a promotional 'Mapped In NY' company map (f4yq-wry5) and the WiredNYC broadband-certification program (3 datasets).
The site itself is not machine-accessible: edc.nyc and nycedc.com return 403 with a Cloudflare challenge to non-browser clients, so even the project/asset/RFP listings can't be reliably scraped.

Net-new write surface: respond to a solicitation (RFPResponse) — register interest or submit a proposal to an EDC RFP/RFEI, which today happens only by email, at a pre-submission conference, or by manual document submission. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Only 5 EDC-labeled datasets: Mapped In NY, NYC Ferry Ridership, and 3 WiredNYC tables. SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. EDC's only real, open, machine-readable surface — and it misses the core business entirely.
Drupal (robots.txt /core/, /profiles/) behind Cloudflare; returns 403 'cf-mitigated: challenge' to non-browser clients. Projects, real estate, and RFPs live here as pages with no API and no JSON.
Alias of edc.nyc; same Cloudflare bot challenge.
Cloudflare + CloudFront. Rider-facing schedules/booking; no documented public API under EDC. Ridership is the one operational dataset EDC publishes to Open Data.
Where formal city procurement/solicitation responses are handled; EDC solicitations are not natively exposed as an API here.

domains.agency: Fire Department of the City of New York (FDNY)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Livesite (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) informational site + FDNY Business on a rented Accela Civic Platform (fires.fdnycloud.org, Cloudflare + Azure App Gateway, Datadog RUM)
domains.tagline: FDNY publishes its firehouses, incidents, inspections, and violations generously on Open Data, but every business transaction is trapped in a rented Accela permitting cloud with no API.
domains.netnew: FirePermitApplication — apply for and track a fire permit / Certificate of Operation / inspection request via FDNY Business
findings: FDNY is a split domain: an informational NYC.gov site (Akamai/nginx/Livesite/Dynatrace) plus FDNY Business (fires.fdnycloud.org), a rented Accela Civic Platform (Citizen Access) permitting application fronted by Cloudflare and an Azure Application Gateway — with no public API.

The reference and operational data is unusually OPEN: 17 NYC Open Data datasets covering firehouses, minute-level Fire and EMS incident dispatch, Bureau of Fire Prevention inspections, active violation orders, certificates of fitness, building vacate lists, and fire-cause investigations.

But the business SERVICE layer has no machine-readable surface: applying for a fire permit, holding a Certificate of Fitness/Operation, scheduling an inspection, or answering a violation happens only inside the rented Accela portal or on paper.
The Open Data cut is historical/snapshot: inspections, violations, certificates, and building summaries are all published as '(Historical)' extracts — the live regulatory state lives inside Accela.
Net-new write surface: submit a fire permit application (FirePermitApplication) — the core FDNY Business transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 17 FDNY datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open FDNY API — reference/operational data only.

FDNY Business — fire permits, Certificates of Fitness/Operation, inspection scheduling, violation response. Accela Citizen Access (.aspx), Datadog RUM; no JSON/OpenAPI. Fronted by Cloudflare + Azure Application Gateway.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
x-akamai-transformed / server-timing on the informational site.
Accela portal ships a Datadog browser-agent CSP report-uri.

domains.agency: NYC Health + Hospitals (H+H)
domains.platform: Epic (MyChart on IIS + live FHIR R4 / SMART on FHIR) behind Radware Bot Manager
domains.tagline: A real health-data standard (Epic FHIR R4 / SMART on FHIR) is already live here — the work is to extend it to booking and open the facility directory, not to invent an API.
domains.netnew: AppointmentRequest — request/book an appointment (no write in the live FHIR surface)
findings: H+H is the FIRST domain in this project with a real, live, standards-based API already in production: an Epic FHIR R4 / SMART on FHIR endpoint (epicproxypda.nychhc.org, 'Epic November 2025') exposing 59 resource types with an OAuth2 authorize/token surface. The health-data standard exists — the work is not to invent an API.

But that FHIR surface is read-mostly and patient-scoped. Appointment is read + search only; there is NO Slot resource and NO $book/$find operation, so BOOKING has no machine-readable contract even inside the standard. Location/Organization/Practitioner exist but require SMART authorization (unauthenticated Location search returns HTTP 401).

There is no OPEN directory. A patient or agent asking 'which H+H hospital near me has cardiology and takes new patients?' has nothing to call — the facility/service/provider directory is HTML on a bot-walled marketing site, not on FHIR (auth-gated) and not on Open Data.

H+H publishes NOTHING to NYC Open Data. Its data is clinical and lives in Epic (HIPAA-protected); the health datasets on data.cityofnewyork.us belong to a different agency, DOHMH. See opendata-hhc.md.

Everything runs on a vendor EHR. The FHIR API, the MyChart portal, and the OAuth surface are all Epic — H+H owns the data but not the contract. The site is protected by Radware Bot Manager, a third vendor.

Net-new write surface: AppointmentRequest — request/book an appointment — the most common patient transaction, with no API today and outside the live FHIR surface. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: THE real H+H API. CapabilityStatement: fhirVersion 4.0.1, software 'Epic November 2025', 59 resource types (Patient, Condition, Observation, Appointment, Location, Organization, Practitioner, MedicationRequest, DocumentReference, ...). OAuth2 authorize/token at /FHIRProxy/oauth2. Appointment is read+search only; no Slot, no $book, no open directory (Location search → 401).

Epic MyChart on Microsoft-IIS/10.0 (MyChartPersistence cookie). The human UI over the same Epic system — where patients see appointments, message providers, refill prescriptions, pay bills. No open API of its own; the machine surface is the FHIR endpoint above.
Epic's public FHIR endpoint directory (480 orgs); lists 'NYC Health + Hospitals' → the FHIRProxy base URL. How the H+H FHIR endpoint is discoverable at all.
Marketing/directory site (locations, services, doctors, help-paying-your-bill). Behind Radware Bot Manager (server: rdwr, 302 to validate.perfdrive.com) — crawl blocked at the edge. No content API.
Health datasets here belong to the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene (DOHMH). H+H owns ZERO datasets. Adjacent, not owned.

domains.agency: NYC Human Resources Administration / Department of Social Services (HRA/DSS)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Livesite (informational) + ACCESS NYC (WordPress/WP Engine, open-source Drools eligibility rules) + ACCESS HRA (React SPA behind Akamai) application portal
domains.tagline: HRA has open caseload data and an open-source eligibility engine, but nothing binds screening, eligibility, and application into one API a resident or agent can call.
domains.netnew: BenefitsApplication — apply for and track SNAP / Cash Assistance / Medicaid, today locked in the ACCESS HRA portal
findings: HRA is a three-surface benefits domain: an informational NYC.gov/Livesite site, the ACCESS NYC eligibility screener (access.nyc.gov, WordPress/WP Engine), and the ACCESS HRA application portal (a069-access.nyc.gov, a React SPA behind Akamai).

The standout finding: ACCESS NYC's eligibility LOGIC is OPEN SOURCE — github.com/NYCOpportunity/ACCESS-NYC-Rules is a public Drools business rules engine driving the screener, alongside a benefits-screening-api. The rules are public; there is still no API that returns a screening result to a caller.

The reference and caseload data is unusually open: 49 NYC Open Data datasets covering SNAP/Cash Assistance/Medicaid recipient and case counts, application-center directories (Benefits Access Centers, SNAP Centers, Medicaid Offices, Homebase), wait times, and 75-column case-action reports.

But the resident TRANSACTION layer has no machine-readable surface: applying for and tracking a benefits case lives only inside the ACCESS HRA React portal or an in-person visit. No dataset and no API exposes an individual application/case (aggregate case-action counts are the closest twin — and never per-resident).

Net-new write surface: apply for benefits (BenefitsApplication) — the core resident transaction, with no API today. The modernization move is to CONNECT the open screener, the open rules, and the closed application step into one owned benefits API + MCP. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 49 HRA datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open HRA API — caseload, directory, and case-action reference data only.

apis_observed.owner: NYC Opportunity (Mayor's Office for Economic Opportunity)
apis_observed.note: The Drools business rules governing the ACCESS NYC eligibility screener at access.nyc.gov/eligibility. Logic is public; no hosted eligibility API returns results to callers. A companion benefits-screening-api repo (2025 replatform) exists.

The open-source ACCESS NYC screener and program catalog (theme 'access'; Cloudflare edge). Program metadata is published as the 'NYC Benefits Platform: Benefits and Programs Dataset' (kvhd-5fmu). Screener returns HTML, not JSON.
ACCESS HRA — apply for SNAP/Cash Assistance/Medicaid, upload documents, view case status. React SPA; Akamai bot protection (_abck/bm_sz); no JSON/OpenAPI surface.
Content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM (livesite-version 22). No content API.

domains.agency: New York City Independent Budget Office (IBO)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Content API v2 SPA (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace/WebTrends); off legacy ibo.nyc.ny.us; 20 Socrata datasets; Airtable/ArcGIS/Infogram embeds
domains.tagline: NYC's nonpartisan budget watchdog already runs a real content API and 20 open datasets, but neither has a documented, agent-native contract.
domains.netnew: DataRequest — an API for the 'Ask IBO' data/analysis request, today a web form / email only
findings: IBO gave up its own domain: the legacy ibo.nyc.ny.us now 301-redirects to www.ibo.nyc.gov — the 'Independent' Budget Office moved its public identity onto the shared NYC.gov chassis.

The site is a client-side SPA on the NYC.gov 'Content API v2' platform (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace/WebTrends). Every path returns the same shell; content is fetched by jQuery from a real but UNDOCUMENTED JSON API at apps.nyc.gov/content-api/v2 — a third distinct NYC.gov platform after NYCHA's Livesite.

IBO's product is analysis, and ~1,145 publications (reports, testimony, letters, budget options, explainers, interactives) already flow through that content API with structured metadata (title, date, topic, publication type, fiscal year, author) — but there is no documented, versioned, agent-native contract over it.

The 20 IBO Open Data datasets are WIDE and pivoted: one row per line-item, one column per fiscal year ('FY 1980' … 'FY 2020'). It is Excel-on-Socrata; a modern API would pivot to long-form (series, lineItem, fiscalYear, value).

As a reference/analysis agency, IBO has NO citizen transaction — no payments, permits, or applications. Its single interactive input is 'Ask IBO' (a data/analysis request), today a web form / email to info@ibo.nyc.gov with no API. That is the net-new WRITE surface (DataRequest).
Data-viz is outsourced: interactives embed Airtable, ArcGIS Online (ibomap.maps.arcgis.com), and Infogram; site search is a Google Custom Search. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Backs the SPA. Returns ~1,145 IBO publications with structured metadata (Title, Publication Date, Topics, Publication Type, Fiscal Year, Author) plus a /nav/ibo tree. Real machine-readable API but no OpenAPI, no versioned consumer contract.
20 IBO datasets under label 'NYC Independent Budget Office (IBO)'. SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. Fiscal time-series, mostly WIDE (one column per fiscal year).
Client-side jQuery/Bootstrap SPA; every route returns the same 36 KB shell. Formerly ibo.nyc.ny.us (301-redirect).
Map interactives embedded via CSP frame-src.
Some IBO interactives are Airtable embeds — data outside IBO's own contract.
Chart/infographic embeds.
On-site search is a Google Custom Search Engine — search rented, not owned.
ruxitagentjs injection + webtrends_v10.js on every page.

domains.agency: New York City Law Department (Office of the Corporation Counsel)
domains.platform: NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' CMS (Akamai + nginx + Dynatrace) — no Law-specific platform, portal, or application system
domains.tagline: The City's in-house law firm publishes seven stale annual snapshots — and the claims dollars that matter belong to the Comptroller.
domains.netnew: LawInternshipApplication — submit a legal internship / fellowship application (the one citizen-initiated transaction with this agency)
findings: The Law Department is an AGENCY-FACING legal office — the City's in-house law firm — with almost no citizen-facing transactions. Its public site runs the same shared NYC.gov 'Livesite' chassis (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) as every other agency; there is no Law-specific portal or application system.

Its Open Data footprint is thin and stale: only 7 datasets under 'Law Department (LAW)', every one flagged 'No' automation and 'Annually' updated — a civil-litigation case index, a list of legal divisions, three parallel publication feeds (press releases / speeches / columns), M/WBE contracting statistics, and a pro bono Public Service Program firm list.

The data that matters most is not the Law Department's to publish: the ledger of claims filed against the City and settlement dollars paid lives with the Office of the Comptroller (ex6k-ym48). The Law Department's own litigation dataset (pjgc-h7uv) is a case INDEX, not the citywide claims record.
There is no service layer and no write surface at all — no API, no OpenAPI, no JSON beyond the SODA endpoints on the seven annual datasets.

Net-new write surface: submit a legal internship / fellowship application (LawInternshipApplication) — the single transaction a member of the public actually initiates with this agency, today handled only via email/PDF instructions on the Careers page. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 7 Law Department datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The only real, open Law API — thin, annual, manually-updated reference data.
content only — About, Divisions, Careers, Public Resources, News. Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API, no application system.
Claims Report — Underlying Settlements and Claims Filed. The authoritative claims/settlement ledger for City litigation; published by the Comptroller, NOT the Law Department.

domains.agency: NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' informational site (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) + Esri ArcGIS Online (Discover NYC Landmarks map) + Salesforce Experience Cloud (Portico permit portal)
domains.tagline: LPC's landmark record is wide open but scattered across three vendor silos — Socrata data, an ArcGIS map, and a Salesforce permit portal — with no owned API binding them and no way to file a permit through code.
domains.netnew: LandmarkPermitApplication — file a Certificate of Appropriateness / No Effect / Minor Work application (today Salesforce Portico only, no API)
findings: LPC is a three-silo domain: an informational NYC.gov 'Livesite' site, the Discover NYC Landmarks map on Esri ArcGIS Online (nyclpc.maps.arcgis.com), and the Portico permit portal on Salesforce Experience Cloud (portico.lpc.nyc.gov/s/) — none of them an owned LPC API.

The reference data is unusually OPEN: 15 NYC Open Data datasets covering individual/interior/scenic landmarks, the Individual Landmark and Historic District Building Database (37 columns of architect/style/material/date), historic districts, designation and archaeology reports, complaints, and violations.

Unlike NYCHA, even the permit record is published — 'LPC Permit Application Information' (dpm2-m9mq, 32 columns) is an open READ twin of issued Certificates of Appropriateness / No Effect. What is missing is the WRITE side.

Filing a new permit application (Certificate of Appropriateness / No Effect / Minor Work) has no API at all — it happens only inside the Salesforce Portico community or on paper. That is the net-new write surface.
Everything is keyed on LP_NUMBER and BBL/BIN, so the three silos can be bound into one owned, LP_NUMBER-keyed contract. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 15 LPC datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open LPC API — designation, building, permit-history, and enforcement data.

apis_observed.public: map UI; ArcGIS REST feature services
apis_observed.note: Discover NYC Landmarks map. Backed by ArcGIS Online hosted feature services (queryable ArcGIS REST), but not documented or owned by LPC as an API.

Portico — file a Certificate of Appropriateness / No Effect / Minor Work application and track its status. Salesforce Lightning/Aura community (/s/ path, force.com, renderCtx/LSKey-c$ cookies); no JSON/OpenAPI surface.
content only — about, designations, permit guide, hearings; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM, AWS ALB. No content API.

domains.agency: Office of the Manhattan Borough President (MBPO)
domains.platform: WordPress (Divi theme) on WP Engine, behind Cloudflare; only API is the default WordPress REST API + Forminator forms
domains.tagline: The Manhattan BP's civic data is already open — just fragmented across 21 one-off datasets, wrapped in a generic WordPress template, and missing any API for the one thing residents most want to do: serve on a community board.
domains.netnew: CommunityBoardApplication — apply to serve on a Manhattan community board (today a Forminator web form / PDF, no API)
findings: Contrary to the hypothesis, the Manhattan BP is not a data desert: it publishes 21 NYC Open Data datasets (agency label 'Manhattan Borough President (MBPO)') covering ULURP recommendations, appointments, community-board leadership, constituent services, and years of funding awards.

But the data is FRAGMENTED — one dataset per program per fiscal year. Capital Grant Awards alone is FIVE separate datasets (2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018), and discretionary funding is further split across Tourism Grants, Community Grants, MCAP, and Police-Community Relations Awards.

The public site (manhattanbp.nyc.gov) is a generic off-the-shelf WordPress/Divi build on WP Engine behind Cloudflare; its only API is the default WordPress REST API, with nothing purpose-built for the office's civic work.

The flagship citizen action — applying to serve on a community board — is a WordPress/Forminator web form (or a downloadable PDF) with no machine-readable contract and no Open Data twin. That is the net-new WRITE surface.

All five borough presidents run near-identical thin sites and publish the same shapes of data under parallel Socrata labels (BPBK, BPBX, QBP, BPSI) — a candidate for ONE federated Borough President API deployed per office rather than five one-off builds. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 21 MBPO datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open MBPO data API — but fragmented into one dataset per program per fiscal year.
Default WP REST surface (wp/v2 pages/posts) plus plugin namespaces (forminator/v1, divi/v1, redirection/v1, google-site-kit/v1). No purpose-built endpoint for the office's civic entities.
The community-board application and other intake live as Forminator forms or downloadable PDFs — HTML forms, no machine-readable write contract.

apis_observed.owner: MBPO (WordPress + Divi theme on WP Engine)
apis_observed.note: Content only; Cloudflare edge, WP Engine hosting, Google Site Kit RUM/SEO. Off-the-shelf Divi build.

domains.agency: Manhattan District Attorney's Office (New York County District Attorney)
domains.platform: WordPress (custom 'dany' theme) behind Sucuri CloudProxy; Jetpack Search; The Events Calendar; WPForms/Contact Form 7; Mailchimp; exposed WP REST API; FOIL delegated to NYC OpenRecords
domains.tagline: A WordPress newsroom whose only API is accidental, no open data at all, FOIL delegated and tips un-built — five identical DA offices waiting on one shared contract.
domains.netnew: TipSubmission — submit a routable tip to a DA bureau (no API today; only a generic reCAPTCHA contact form). FOIL is delegated off-site to NYC OpenRecords.
findings: The Manhattan DA publishes NO NYC Open Data — as an independently elected county prosecutor it sits outside the mayoral open-data program; four agency-label queries against the Socrata Discovery API return zero datasets.
The only genuinely machine-readable object is accidental: the newsroom (~3,029 posts) is JSON solely because WordPress ships a REST API (wp/v2/posts). It is undocumented, unversioned, content-only.

Nothing the office actually does has an API — prosecutions, bureaus/initiatives, victim services, and tips exist only as HTML pages or prose; reporting suspected wrongdoing means a generic reCAPTCHA contact form or a phone call.
Two core workflows are outsourced or un-built: FOIL records requests are delegated off-site to NYC OpenRecords, and tip intake has no structured surface at all.

All five NYC District Attorneys run the same functions on the same kind of stack — this is a candidate for ONE shared DA API rather than five silos. Net-new write surface: submit a tip (TipSubmission). See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: The one real API. wp/v2/posts returns the newsroom as JSON, x-wp-total: 3029 (press releases, media coverage, op-eds, reports, testimony). Accidental (WP default), undocumented, content-only.
Community events feed via the Modern Tribe Events Calendar plugin.
On-site search delegated to Jetpack Search; not owned by the office.
Generic contact form is the closest thing to tip intake — no schema, no structured API.
The office delegates all FOIL requests here; its statutory intake workflow lives off its own domain. No DA-owned API.
Newsletter signup posts to Mailchimp.
server: Sucuri/Cloudproxy fronts the whole site.
reCAPTCHA gates forms; exactmetrics/v1 and wordfence/v1 REST namespaces present.

domains.agency: NYC Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice (MOCJ)
domains.platform: WordPress (WP Engine) behind Cloudflare; Kadence/ACF/Contact Form 7 + accidental wp/v2 REST API
domains.tagline: A coordination office that owns almost no data and routes people between courts, agencies, and community programs with PDFs and outbound links — the fix is to give that routing a machine-readable contract.
domains.netnew: ProgramReferral — route a person or court case to a MOCJ-coordinated program
findings: MOCJ is a split domain: a thin nyc.gov/site/criminaljustice stub (NYC.gov Livesite) whose subpages 404, and the real office site at criminaljustice.cityofnewyork.us — WordPress on WP Engine behind Cloudflare, a fifth distinct platform after Parks' Smarty/PHP, DOE's Sitefinity/.NET, Council's WordPress, and NYCHA's Livesite+Siebel.

It is a policy/convening/grant-making office that OWNS almost no data: NYC Open Data carries exactly ONE MOCJ dataset — Supervised Release Dockets (atne-2dki, 6 columns, last updated 2023, ~1,074 views).

The jail-population numbers MOCJ is best known for are not MOCJ's. Its System Data page publishes PDF 'explainers' (jail-population increase, re-arrest rates) and links OUT to DOC (Daily Population in Custody), NYPD (CompStat), NYS DCJS, and the Board of Correction. MOCJ convenes and analyzes; it does not own the metrics.

The only machine-readable surface MOCJ has is ACCIDENTAL: the WordPress wp/v2 REST API, which exposes programs, solicitations, notices, reports, briefs, and data stories as JSON — undocumented, unintended, and not a product. A Contact Form 7 REST endpoint is the only write path, and it is a generic web form.

Net-new write surface: a ProgramReferral — routing a person or court case to a MOCJ-coordinated program along the Sequential Intercept Model. This is the office's core function, yet it has no API; referrals happen offline or via a generic web form. Referral data is sensitive and must be gated. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Undocumented but fully functional — returns programs (26), solicitation (25), notice (26), reports (26), data_reports (109), briefs (54), data_stories (6), team (92) as JSON. Not designed or published as an API.
The only write path — generic form-to-email (vendor enrollment, contact). Not a transactional/data API.
Exactly ONE MOCJ dataset: Supervised Release Dockets (atne-2dki). SODA /resource/atne-2dki.json.
Thin stub on the NYC.gov chassis (Akamai, nginx, Dynatrace/mPulse); most subpages 404 — the real site moved to criminaljustice.cityofnewyork.us.
MOCJ's System Data page links OUT to DOC Daily Population in Custody, NYPD CompStat 2.0, NYS DCJS, and Board of Correction for the jail/crime numbers it analyzes.
cf-ray, __cf_bm; fronts the WP Engine origin.

domains.agency: NYC Mayor's Office of Media & Entertainment (MOME)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' informational site (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) + a login-walled ASP.NET Core 'MOME E-Apply' film-permit portal on the shared nyceventpermits.nyc.gov event-permitting platform
domains.tagline: The output is the most-viewed dataset in the city; the application is trapped behind a portal login.
domains.netnew: FilmPermitApplication — apply for a film/photography permit via API instead of the login-walled E-Apply portal
findings: MOME is a split domain: an informational NYC.gov/Livesite site (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) plus a film-permit application system — 'MOME E-Apply', an ASP.NET Core app at nyceventpermits.nyc.gov/film — that is login-walled with no API.

The flagship OUTPUT is heroically open: 'Film Permits' (tg4x-b46p) is one of the most-viewed datasets in all of NYC Open Data (~530k lifetime views), automated and updated daily — every location shoot the Film Office greenlights, as a public geography record.

But the INTAKE is closed: applying for a permit (the everyday production transaction) lives only inside the E-Apply portal or a Letter-in-Lieu email. The applicant / production company is never published — only the issued permit is.

MOME's Open Data footprint is unusually narrow: just 2 assets under its label — Film Permits and MARCH nightlife inspections (b84a-xy2t, via the Office of Nightlife). The rest of MOME (Made in NY, workforce, Public Access Media) is HTML only.

Net-new write surface: apply_for_film_permit (FilmPermitApplication) — the one transaction that turns MOME's open output into an owned, agent-native front door. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 2 MOME datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. Film Permits (tg4x-b46p) is the flagship, daily-automated feed of issued permits — the one real, open MOME API. MARCH inspections (b84a-xy2t) is the other.

apis_observed.owner: MOME (ASP.NET Core app on shared event-permit platform)
apis_observed.note: 'MOME E-Apply' — Microsoft-IIS/10.0, X-Powered-By ASP.NET, .AspNetCore.Antiforgery cookie, /Web/Login redirect. Where productions actually apply for permits; no JSON/OpenAPI. Shared platform also serves CECM/DOT event permits.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. Permit instructions, programs, Public Access Media, Nightlife. No content API.

'Filming Permits - Transportation Department' (c2az-nhru, ~90k views) — the same permits from DOT's street-work angle, WITH applicant contacts, fees, milestones, and lat/long that MOME's own feed omits.
x-akamai-transformed on the informational site.
x-oneagent-js-injection real-user monitoring.

domains.agency: NYC Emergency Management (NYCEM)
domains.platform: Informational site on shared NYC.gov Livesite (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace); Notify NYC alerting rented from Everbridge (a858-nycnotify.nyc.gov + feeds.everbridge.net CAP RSS feed)
domains.tagline: NYCEM publishes its incidents, evacuation zones, and hazard plans on Open Data and even emits a real machine-readable Notify NYC CAP alert feed — but that feed and the alert subscription both live on a rented Everbridge platform, not an owned city API.
domains.netnew: NotifyNYCSubscription — subscribe to Notify NYC emergency alerts by channel, location, and category through an owned write API
findings: NYCEM is a rented-alerting domain: an informational NYC.gov site (Akamai/nginx/Livesite/Dynatrace) plus Notify NYC — the city's flagship emergency-alert product — running on a rented Everbridge mass-notification SaaS.

Unusually, the alert stream IS machine-readable: Notify NYC emits a genuine CAP (Common Alerting Protocol) RSS feed on Everbridge (feeds.everbridge.net), republishing each alert across ~14 languages with a per-message CAP XML document. But that feed — and the subscription — are vendor-hosted, not an owned city API.

Reference and situational data is well published: 13 NYC Open Data datasets covering emergency response incidents (107k views), hurricane inundation/evacuation zones and centers, the NYCEM Emergency Notifications archive, the Hazard Mitigation Plan, and community-preparedness programs (CERT, Ready NY, Partners in Preparedness).
The gap is the WRITE side: enrolling in Notify NYC (NotifyNYCSubscription) has no public API — a resident subscribes only through the Everbridge Member Portal, an SMS keyword, or a call to 311.

Live operational status — which cooling centers / emergency supply shelters are open right now — is published only through NYCEM's seasonal Finder tools, not a machine-readable status feed. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Notify NYC Common Alerting Protocol RSS feed. Real, machine-readable, multilingual (~14 languages), each item linking a per-message CAP XML enclosure. The one live NYCEM alert API — but vendor-hosted, read-only, no owned subscription write.
13 NYCEM datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. Incidents, evacuation zones/centers, notifications archive, hazard mitigation, CERT / Ready NY. Reference & situational data only.
where residents enroll in Notify NYC; server-rendered, no public JSON/OpenAPI subscribe endpoint. Redirects deflect probes for a subscription API.
content only — Ready NY, Know Your Zone, hazards. Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
live cooling-center / evacuation-center openings; map UI, no documented status API.

domains.agency: New York Public Library (NYPL)
domains.platform: NYPL-owned open-source stack — Ruby on Rails Digital Collections API (api.repo.nypl.org), PHP Slim Locations API (refinery.nypl.org), Node/AWS discovery-api + Sierra ILS, accessibility-first React design system; site behind Imperva Incapsula
domains.tagline: NYPL is the counter-example: an independent nonprofit that already built and open-sourced genuinely good public APIs for its collections, locations, and catalog.
domains.netnew: Hold (BookHold) — place and track a reservation on a catalog item via a clean, agent-native public API (backed by NYPL's existing internal hold services)
findings: NYPL is the counter-example of this project: an independent nonprofit (serving Manhattan, the Bronx, and Staten Island) that has already built and operated genuinely good PUBLIC APIs for years — where nearly every city agency traps its data in HTML or a vendor CRM.

The Digital Collections API (api.repo.nypl.org/api/v2) is a real, token-authenticated public API over millions of digitized items — items/search, items/{uuid}, MODS metadata, captures, rights, and collections; built on Ruby on Rails.

The Locations API (refinery.nypl.org/api/nypl/locations/v1.0) is an OPEN, hypermedia (HAL) API publishing every branch with hours, amenities, accessibility, and links to events, exhibitions, and alerts; built on PHP Slim.

NYPL open-sources nearly its entire stack at github.com/NYPL — the Research Catalog discovery-api, an accessibility-first React design system (nypl-design-system), the nypl-core ontology, a web-based ebook reader, and patron/hold/Sierra microservices. Ownership is exemplary.

NYC Open Data holds only 6 thin, mostly-stale NYPL datasets (2010-2011 branch-services stats + a facilities layer). NYPL's living data is on its own APIs, not NYC Open Data — a real finding, and the inverse of every other domain.

The remaining gaps are unification and one write: the three good APIs are separate contracts with different auth/hosts/shapes, and placing a HOLD (reservation) on a book — though backed by real internal services (on-site-hold-request-service, patron-eligibility-service, nypl-hold-request-consumer) — has no clean, documented, agent-native public API. See apis-observed.md and tech-stack.md.

apis_observed.note: Digital Collections API. items/search, items/{uuid}, items/mods/{uuid}, items/rights/{uuid}, collections, collections/{uuid}. Returns 401 'HTTP Basic: Access denied' without a registered token. Ruby on Rails + Phusion Passenger + nginx. A real, mature NYPL-owned API.

Locations API. locations, locations/{id}, plus HAL _links to /events, /exhibitions, /alerts, /amenities, /blogs. Rich payload: hours_data, geolocation, access, contacts, fundraising. PHP Slim (NYPL\Refinery\Server). Fully open.
The primary API supporting the NYPL Research Catalog. Node/AWS. Backed by the Sierra ILS (ruby-sierra-api-client) and a stream of patron/hold microservices.

nypl-design-system (accessibility-first React, 80 stars), web-reader (ebook reader), nypl-core (ontology), on-site-hold-request-service, nypl-hold-request-consumer, patron-eligibility-service, dgx-patron-creator-service, nypl-library-card-app, barcode-service. Exemplary open ownership.
Patron-facing site + Research Catalog UI. Edge-obscured by Incapsula; front end uses the NYPL design system. Where patrons place holds today, inside an authenticated account.
Only 6 NYPL datasets, mostly stale 2010-2011 branch-services stats + a facilities layer. Not where NYPL's real data lives.

domains.agency: NYC Office of Administrative Trials & Hearings (OATH)
domains.platform: NYC.gov Livesite (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace) informational site + a legacy Apache Struts / Oracle WebLogic ECB Ticket Finder portal
domains.tagline: OATH publishes every ECB summons, hearing, and decision as open data, but the one thing a respondent needs to do — respond to a summons — is trapped in a legacy Struts portal with no API.
domains.netnew: SummonsDispute — respond to / dispute a summons (request a hearing) via an owned API
findings: OATH is a split domain: an informational NYC.gov site (Akamai/nginx/Livesite v22/Dynatrace) plus the ECB Ticket Finder / Respond-to-a-Summons portal (a820-ecbticketfinder.nyc.gov), a legacy Apache Struts 2 app on Oracle WebLogic (.action, X-ORACLE-DMS-ECID, WebLogic JSESSIONID) with no API.

The adjudication data is exceptionally OPEN: the 'OATH Hearings Division Case Status' dataset (jz4z-kudi, 74 columns, updated DAILY, ~400k lifetime views) publishes every ECB summons — charges, respondent, violation location, hearing, decision, penalty, and balance — plus 'OATH Trials Division Case Status' (y3hw-z6bm) for the tribunal side.

But the respondent SERVICE layer has no machine-readable surface: looking up and RESPONDING to a summons — dispute it, request or reschedule a hearing, submit a defense, reopen a default — is only possible through the legacy Struts portal, by mail, or in person.
OATH adjudicates but does not issue: the summonses come from DOB, DSNY, FDNY, DOHMH, DEP, DCWP and others; unpaid decisions become docketed judgments collected by the Department of Finance.
Net-new write surface: SummonsDispute — respond to / dispute a summons (request a hearing) — the single most common respondent transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 2 OATH datasets — Hearings Division Case Status (jz4z-kudi, 74c, daily) and Trials Division Case Status (y3hw-z6bm). SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open OATH API — case-status data only.

Look up a summons by ticket number or respondent and respond/dispute it. Server-rendered Struts 2 (.action), Oracle WebLogic (X-ORACLE-DMS-ECID, JSESSIONID '!'), ISO-8859-1; no JSON/OpenAPI. search.action + getViolationbyID.action; form fields searchViolationObject.violationNo / respondentName / borough.
content only — hearings, defaults, payments/penalties, reopen-a-default. Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
NYC.gov ID / self-service sign-in umbrella.

domains.agency: NYC Office of Chief Medical Examiner (OCME)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' shared chassis (Akamai + nginx + AWS ALB + Dynatrace) — informational only; no OCME application. Transactions run on paper (notarized) forms + NYC 311.
domains.tagline: The least-instrumented, most privacy-constrained agency: one stale dataset, no application, and a core object that can never be published.
domains.netnew: DeathRecordRequest — request and track a decedent's medical-examiner casefile record/report
findings: OCME is the most data-dark agency assessed: exactly ONE dataset carries its NYC Open Data label — 'Monthly Indicators' (8r6c-ydwk), a Mayor's Management Report passthrough whose real months run only July 2015 - May 2016. It is not casework.
There is no OCME application at all — no portal, no CRM, no login to fingerprint. The records-request service layer is a notarized paper form and NYC 311, with a three-to-six-month turnaround.
The core object — a death investigation — is confidential by law and is never published, not per-decedent and not in aggregate. Any responsible surface must stay non-identifying.
What little is public is federated elsewhere: missing-persons visibility lives in federal NamUs, and death certificates are issued by DOHMH. OCME owns almost none of its own digital footprint.

Net-new write surface: request a decedent's medical-examiner casefile record (DeathRecordRequest) — the core family transaction, today a notarized PDF plus a 311 case. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: Exactly one OCME dataset — 'Monthly Indicators' (8r6c-ydwk), a stale MMR passthrough (real months Jul 2015 - May 2016). Not casework. The only OCME-labeled machine-readable asset.
The de facto records-request/service channel for OCME; linked from every OCME service page. Not an OCME system.
Where OCME's Identification Unit surfaces missing-persons and unidentified-decedent cases. OCME participates; does not own it.
Content only; Akamai edge, nginx, AWS ALB, Dynatrace RUM. No content API and no application behind it.

domains.agency: NYC Office of Technology & Innovation (OTI)
domains.platform: NYC.gov 'Livesite' informational site (Akamai + nginx + mPulse + Dynatrace); OTI OPERATES NYC Open Data (Socrata/Tyler), the api.nyc.gov gateway (Microsoft Azure API Management; GeoClient/GeoSearch), and the 311 pipeline.
domains.tagline: The agency that runs the city's data infrastructure, made into a first-class API product.
domains.netnew: One owned, unified gateway + catalog API — plus two write surfaces: register a dataset, and request an api.nyc.gov gateway key.
findings: OTI is the META case: it is not an agency with data trapped in HTML — it OPERATES the city's data infrastructure. It runs NYC Open Data (data.cityofnewyork.us on Socrata/Tyler), the api.nyc.gov gateway (Microsoft Azure API Management, hosting GeoClient and GeoSearch), NYC.gov itself, LinkNYC, the municipal broadband program, public Wi-Fi, and the 311 pipeline.

As a publisher it is enormous: 221 datasets under the 'Office of Technology and Innovation (OTI)' label — including the most-viewed dataset in the entire city (311 Service Requests, erm2-nwe9, 1.26M+ views), plus the catalog OF ITSELF (the LL251 Published Data Asset Inventory 5tqd-u88y and the Open Data Release Tracker qj2z-ibhs), LinkNYC, broadband, Wi-Fi, planimetric base-map, and .nyc domain data.

The api.nyc.gov gateway is real and running (Azure APIM) but has no owned, machine-readable catalog of its services and no self-service key API — GeoClient returns HTTP 401 'missing subscription key' with keys provisioned only through a developer portal.

The reframe is unique: because the data is already open and the gateway already exists, the modernization is not to LIBERATE data but to PRODUCTIZE the operator role — a single owned gateway + catalog API that lets a developer or agent discover a dataset, discover a gateway service, geocode an address, and register an asset through one contract. OTI is the natural home for the project's nyc-commons + registry ideas.

Net-new write surfaces: register a dataset to the catalog, and request an api.nyc.gov gateway key — the two things a platform operator should offer programmatically but today does only through internal or portal workflows. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.owner: NYC OTI (on Microsoft Azure API Management)
apis_observed.note: The citywide API gateway OTI operates. Request-Context appId + 'missing subscription key' fingerprint Azure APIM. Hosts GeoClient (address/BBL/BIN geocoding) and other agency APIs. No owned catalog of services, no self-service key API.

apis_observed.owner: NYC (DCP Planning Labs; wraps OTI GeoSupport)
apis_observed.note: Open Pelias/Mapzen geocoder over NYC GeoSupport — returns coordinates + geography spine. The open twin of GeoClient. HTTP 200, no key.

The open-data platform OTI operates for the WHOLE city. SODA /resource/<id>.json per dataset + the Socrata Discovery/catalog API. OTI itself publishes 221 datasets, incl. the catalog of the entire corpus.
Content only — About OTI, services, initiatives. Akamai edge, nginx, mPulse + Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
NYC.gov ID / citywide identity — itself an OTI-run platform.
server-timing ak_p / mPulse on the OTI site.
x-oneagent-js-injection real-user monitoring.

domains.agency: New York City Public Advocate (Office of the Public Advocate)
domains.platform: Self-hosted nginx/1.18.0 (Ubuntu) main site (502/down during crawl) + an orphan Next.js/Vercel product (the Worst Landlord Watchlist)
domains.tagline: The city's ombudsman publishes zero open data, runs a fragile self-hosted site, and takes complaints only by web form or phone — give it an owned API to respond.
domains.netnew: OmbudsmanComplaint — file and track a 'help with a city agency' case against a NYC agency
findings: The Public Advocate publishes ZERO datasets to NYC Open Data. Unlike NYCHA (24 datasets), the city's ombudsman has no machine-readable reference data at all — its output is reports/PDFs, its own website, and one product (the Worst Landlord Watchlist).

The main site is self-hosted and fragile. advocate.nyc.gov runs on the Office's own nginx/1.18.0 (Ubuntu) origin — not the shared NYC.gov 'Livesite' platform every other agency uses — and returned 502 Bad Gateway throughout the crawl. The city's watchdog site was literally down.

The one modern, data-backed surface is an orphan. The Worst Landlord Watchlist (landlordwatchlist.com) is a separate Next.js/React app on Vercel with an UNDOCUMENTED /api/landlords JSON endpoint (99 landlords: org, officer, buildings, units, DOB violations, avg open HPD violations, tax liens, evictions, rank) — real data, no docs, no OpenAPI, on infrastructure disconnected from the main site.

The core ombudsman function has no intake API. 'Help with a city agency' — the Public Advocate's constitutional reason for existing — is a web form / phone / email only. There is no machine-readable way to file or track an OmbudsmanComplaint.

Legislation the PA sponsors lives in someone else's system. The Public Advocate introduces bills through the City Council; those files live in Council's Legistar, owned by NYCC, not the PA. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.
Net-new write surface: file a complaint against a city agency (OmbudsmanComplaint) — the single most common resident interaction with the office, with no API today.

apis_observed.note: The one real API observed. Returns { results: [ { landlordid, org, officer, buildings, UNIT, dob, tax_lien, evictions, num_avg, rank } ×99 ] } — the Worst Landlord Watchlist. No docs, no OpenAPI, no versioning; a Next.js data route, not a governed contract.
Self-run nginx/1.18.0 (Ubuntu). NOT the NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' chassis. Returned 502 Bad Gateway; reports, help-with-a-city-agency, and public-interest content are HTML/PDF only, no content API.
Where the PA's sponsored bills actually live. Owned by the Council, not the Public Advocate — the PA is a sponsor, not the system of record.

The underlying HPD housing-maintenance violations / DOB / registration data that the Watchlist is derived from is open here — but NONE of it is published under a Public Advocate label; the PA contributes zero assets.
server: Vercel, x-vercel-cache, x-vercel-id on landlordwatchlist.com — the flagship product is hosted off-platform on a commercial PaaS.

domains.agency: Office of the Queens Borough President (QBP)
domains.platform: WordPress (Divi + Elementor) on WP Engine behind Cloudflare; Yoast + Wordfence; queensbp.org 301s to www.queensbp.nyc.gov
domains.tagline: A thin WordPress brochure with its REST API switched on but empty, and almost no open data — the fix is one shared Borough President API, not a fifth one-off.
domains.netnew: CommunityBoardApplication — apply to serve on a Queens community board (today an HTML/PDF form)
findings: queensbp.org 301-redirects to www.queensbp.nyc.gov — a WordPress/Divi brochure site on WP Engine behind Cloudflare (Yoast, Wordfence, Google Language Translator). A fifth distinct platform after Parks (Smarty/PHP), DOE (Sitefinity/.NET), Council (WordPress), and NYCHA (NYC.gov Livesite + Oracle Siebel).

The WordPress REST API (/wp-json/wp/v2) is switched ON but effectively EMPTY: 0 posts and 0 'project' custom-post-type entries. The one machine-readable surface the platform already ships is dormant because content is authored as Divi page-builder Pages, not structured posts.

Open Data is nearly absent but NOT zero: exactly TWO datasets attributed to 'Queens Borough President (QBP)', both about community boards — Queens Community Board Members (rps4-dwwk, 4 cols) and Queens Community Board District Managers and Chairs (8z5h-tzdr, 20 cols).

The core borough-president acts have NO machine-readable contract: advisory land-use (ULURP) recommendations, tens of millions in annual discretionary funding to nonprofits/schools/cultural groups, events, and press releases all live only as HTML.

Net-new write surface: apply to serve on a community board (CommunityBoardApplication) — the QBP's flagship participatory act ('2026 Queens Community Board Application Process'), today only an HTML/PDF form. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.
Reframe: all five borough presidents run near-identical thin sites. The real low-hanging fruit is ONE shared Borough President API standard implemented per borough, not five one-off builds.

apis_observed.note: Enabled and publicly reachable, returns valid JSON for pages, but X-WP-Total=0 for posts and the 'project' custom post type. A real API surface carrying almost no structured content. Namespaces include wp/v2, oembed, yoast/v1, divi/v1, wpe/cache-plugin, wordfence/v1.

Two QBP-attributed datasets (rps4-dwwk community board members; 8z5h-tzdr district managers and chairs). SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The only substantive machine-readable QBP data — both about community boards.
Land use, community boards, budget/major allocations, constituent services, newsroom — all Divi page-builder Pages; content only, no content API in practice.
301-redirects to www.queensbp.nyc.gov (Cloudflare); the office has migrated onto the nyc.gov domain.

domains.agency: Queens County District Attorney
domains.platform: WordPress 7.0.1 (Divi + Beaver Builder + WPML) on Kinsta behind Cloudflare; FacetWP search; self-hosted Matomo
domains.tagline: A prosecutor's office that publishes 1,200+ press releases and, by accident, already has an API — WordPress REST — but no designed contract, no open data, and no way in.
domains.netnew: TipSubmission — submit a tip, cold-case lead, or FOIL request (no inbound channel today)
findings: Queens DA is a new distinct pattern: it publishes prolifically (1,557 WordPress posts, 1,216 press releases) and that content is ALREADY machine-readable — but through an ACCIDENTAL API (the fully-exposed WordPress REST API, wp-json/wp/v2), not a designed one.
There is NO NYC Open Data corpus for a District Attorney — verified zero Socrata datasets across four agency labels. DAs are county/state prosecutorial agencies and do not publish to NYC Open Data.

The prosecution lifecycle (arraignment 131 → charges 127 → indictment 119 → court-case 378 → conviction) exists only as press-release prose tagged by WordPress category; there is no structured Case resource.

The one genuinely structured dataset is trapped in prose: the cold-case / unidentified-persons initiative encodes NamUs case numbers, sex, age range, and date/location found inside post TITLES (e.g. 'MALE 4-12-2014 NAMUS UP13060').
There is NO inbound write channel at all — tips, cold-case leads, and FOIL records requests go by phone or a static contact page. That is the net-new WRITE surface.
All five NYC borough DA offices run the same functions on similar WordPress stacks — this is the strongest candidate yet for ONE shared, five-borough DA API rather than five accidental REST APIs.

apis_observed.note: The WordPress REST API is fully exposed: 1,557 posts, 60 pages, categories, tags, media. Machine-readable but undesigned, uncontracted, and unofficial — press releases, court-case posts, and cold-case posts all readable as JSON. The de facto Queens DA API.
FacetWP powers on-site faceted search; its /refresh and /fetch endpoints are exposed under wp-json.
Self-hosted Matomo (first-party analytics) integrated into WordPress — notable choice for a public agency over pure third-party tracking.
WPML drives the multilingual site (translation state for 15+ strings); explains per-language content.
Google Site Kit and Yoast SEO REST namespaces present — third-party analytics/SEO tooling.
server: cloudflare; x-kinsta-cache / ki-edge headers; x-redirect-by: WordPress. Managed WordPress on Kinsta behind Cloudflare.
NO Queens DA presence — zero datasets across four agency-label queries. DAs do not publish to NYC Open Data.
Freedom of Information Law requests are the formal data-access path; handled manually via the Resources page. No API.

domains.agency: Queens Public Library (QPL)
domains.platform: Drupal site (behind F5 BIG-IP WAF) + BiblioCommons catalog + Communico events + OverDrive/Libby/Axis 360/hoopla digital + Springshare help
domains.tagline: An independent nonprofit whose every patron surface is a separate vendor SaaS silo, with only its branch directory open.
domains.netnew: BookHold — place a hold/reservation on a catalog item (also: library-card application)
findings: Queens Public Library is an INDEPENDENT NONPROFIT, not a city agency — so it publishes almost nothing to NYC Open Data: a single dataset, Queens Library Branches (kh3d-xhq7), under the agency label 'Queens Library (QBPL)'.

Everything a patron actually uses is composed from packaged SaaS silos, each with its own login and its own vendor API: the BiblioCommons catalog, Communico events, and the OverDrive/Libby, Axis 360, and hoopla digital-lending platforms. None is an owned QPL API.
The public site runs on Drupal behind an F5 BIG-IP WAF that rejects even a polite browser-UA crawl of content pages — the site itself has no public API and actively resists machine access.

The patron TRANSACTIONS — placing a hold, tracking holds, applying for a card — have no machine-readable contract at all; they live only behind a BiblioCommons login. Placing a hold (BookHold) is the net-new write surface.

The three NYC public library systems — Queens, Brooklyn (BPL), and The New York Public Library (NYPL) — are separate nonprofits with separate catalogs, cards, and vendor stacks. There is no shared NYC library API; that absence is the opportunity.

apis_observed.note: Exactly ONE QPL dataset — Queens Library Branches (kh3d-xhq7, 21c) under agency 'Queens Library (QBPL)'. The only open, owned-ish QPL data; branch directory only.

apis_observed.public: public search UI; login for account
apis_observed.note: The catalog, patron account, holds, and card live here. x-version 'nerf15 9.35.0'. A BiblioCommons partner API + widgets/RSS exist, but there is no owned QPL API. Holds and cards are behind login.
Server: Communico. All programs/events; embedded on the queenslibrary.org calendar. Communico exposes a vendor API, not an owned QPL contract.
eBooks/eAudiobooks/eMagazines. Companion silos: Axis 360 and hoopla. Each has its own vendor API and its own catalog; none federated by QPL.
LibAnswers (x-backend springyaws.com); QPL's help/FAQ knowledge base. Vendor-owned.
Drupal (taxonomy/term URL scheme, /core/ paths). F5 BIG-IP ASM WAF rejects browser-UA crawls of content pages. No content API.

domains.agency: NYC Department of Small Business Services (SBS)
domains.platform: Informational site on the NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' chassis (Akamai/nginx/Dynatrace); the transactional MyCity Business portal on Spring (Java) + Adobe AEM + jQuery/Handlebars, hosting the Step-by-Step licensing wizard and certification/enrollment flows.
domains.tagline: SBS publishes who got certified and where to hire, but hides the navigation — the wizard, eligibility, and certification that are the agency's real job.
domains.netnew: MWBECertificationApplication — apply for M/WBE (also EBE/LBE) certification; no API today, only the MyCity Business portal.
findings: SBS is a split domain: an informational NYC.gov site (Akamai/nginx/Livesite/Dynatrace) plus a business service portal — MyCity Business (nyc-business.nyc.gov) — a stateful Spring/Adobe-AEM application with no API.

The program data is reasonably OPEN: 28 NYC Open Data datasets covering the SBS Certified Business List (M/WBE, 56 columns), Business Improvement Districts (directory, maps, and a 64-column FY24 trends report), Workforce1 recruitment events and job listings, service-center locations, and business-incentive rolls.

But SBS is a NAVIGATOR agency, and its navigation is locked: the Step-by-Step licensing wizard (which licenses/permits does my business need?), incentive eligibility, and the M/WBE certification and Workforce1 enrollment flows live only inside the session-bound MyCity Business portal — none has a machine-readable surface.
The public 'SBS Certified Business List' is the shadow of a closed flow: you can read who got certified, but there is no API to apply.
Net-new write surface: apply for M/WBE certification (MWBECertificationApplication) — the flagship SBS business transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 28 SBS datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open SBS API — program/directory data only.

MyCity Business — Step-by-Step licensing wizard (/nycbusiness/wizard), incentive/eligibility lookups, M/WBE certification and Workforce1 enrollment. Spring SESSION cookie, jQuery/Handlebars, JavaScript-only, no JSON/OpenAPI.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
loaded by MyCity Business (cors.js) for content authoring.

domains.agency: NYC School Construction Authority (SCA)
domains.platform: DotNetNuke (DNN) ASP.NET CMS (obvio skin) behind AWS ALB, content in Azure Blob; login-walled bidset SharePoint vendor portal
domains.tagline: SCA opens its whole capital pipeline and its approved-vendor list as data, but the act of becoming a vendor stays locked in a SharePoint login.
domains.netnew: VendorPrequalification — apply to become a prequalified SCA vendor
findings: SCA is a vendor-facing agency on a fifth distinct platform: a DotNetNuke (DNN) ASP.NET CMS (obvio skin) behind an AWS load balancer with content in Azure Blob Storage — after Parks' Smarty/PHP, DOE's Sitefinity/.NET, Council's WordPress, and NYCHA's Livesite + Oracle Siebel.

The reference data is unusually OPEN and procurement-rich: 43 NYC Open Data datasets covering the capital plan (active projects, schedules and budgets, capacity by school), the forward pipeline (upcoming CIP/CAP contracts, current and anticipated RFPs, change orders), the approved-vendor roster (prequalified and disqualified firms), enrollment/capacity demand, and inspections.

But the VENDOR TRANSACTION layer is locked: bid documents and the prequalification workflow live only behind the login-walled bidset.nycsca.org SharePoint portal and PDF forms. The thing a vendor most wants to do — get prequalified and transact on a bid — has no machine-readable contract.
SCA publishes the RESULT of prequalification (the Prequalified Firms roster, szkz-syh6) as Open Data, but never the ACT of applying — a clean read/write mismatch.
The /WS/Reports/ path looks like a web service but returns DNN-rendered HTML; there is no JSON/OpenAPI surface anywhere on the site. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.
Net-new write surface: apply to become a prequalified vendor (VendorPrequalification) — the everyday vendor onboarding transaction, with no API today.

apis_observed.note: 43 SCA datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open SCA API — capital-plan, procurement, vendor-roster, capacity, and inspection reference data.
Online Bidsets — bid documents and the vendor transaction layer. _layouts/OnlineBidsetsLogin/CustomLogin.aspx custom login; no JSON/OpenAPI.
Looks like a web service ('WS') but returns a rendered DNN HTML page, not JSON. No machine-readable contract.
Capital plan, business/vendor guidance, RFPs. DNN CMS behind AWS ALB; content in Azure Blob (dnnhh5cc1.blob.core.windows.net). No content API.
PDF general conditions, safety manuals, press — served via DNNFileManagerPolicy SAS signatures.

domains.agency: Office of the Staten Island Borough President (BPSI)
domains.platform: Weebly brochure site behind Cloudflare (Weebly Forms + Constant Contact); no CMS of record, no API
domains.tagline: The thinnest domain yet: a Weebly brochure site, two trivial datasets, and none of the borough president's charter work available as data.
domains.netnew: CommunityBoardApplication — apply to serve on a Staten Island community board (today an opaque Weebly form)
findings: The Office of the Staten Island Borough President has the thinnest technical footprint assessed so far: a Weebly brochure site (statenislandusa.com) behind Cloudflare, with no CMS of record, no application, and no API.

Only two NYC Open Data assets carry the agency label 'Staten Island Borough President (BPSI)': 'BP Assist Helpline Requests' (3fes-huds), an aggregate count-by-request-type table that ends at FY20, and 'Category Master File' (mmut-uup9), a zero-column blob.

None of the borough president's actual NYC Charter functions — advisory ULURP land-use recommendations, community board appointments, discretionary capital/expense funding, and Borough Board resolutions — is published as data anywhere the office owns; they exist only as PDFs, HTML, or inside other agencies' systems (City Planning ZAP, the adopted budget Schedule C).

The office's two real transactions are un-contracted: BP Assist (report a pothole/litter/graffiti issue) is a web widget, and the Community Board Membership Application is an opaque Weebly form posting to weebly.com/apps/formSubmit.php.

Net-new write surface: apply to a community board (CommunityBoardApplication), plus a BP Assist constituent request. The strategic finding is that all five NYC borough-president offices are near-identical thin sites, making this a candidate for one shared, federated Borough President API keyed by borough — not five bespoke builds.

apis_observed.note: Only 2 BPSI assets: 3fes-huds (aggregate BP Assist counts, ends FY20) and mmut-uup9 (zero-column file). A SODA /resource/3fes-huds.json endpoint exists but the office does not consume it.
Where the Community Board Membership Application posts. Opaque _u… field names; not an API, not documented, not agent-usable.
Newsletter capture.
Weebly built-in site search; no JSON contract.
Content only; no content API, no JSON, no OpenAPI.
